EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms across north central Florida produced gusty winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident of Levy County reported hail up to quarter size and wind gusts around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms moved north through west central Florida on the morning of the 30th. One of the storms produced a brief tornado touchdown east of Arcadia, destroying a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DeSoto County emergency management reported a barn was destroyed by a brief tornado touchdown east of Arcadia. There were 5 antique cars in the barn at the time that were not damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in northeastern Hillsborough county, with some sites reporting over 7 inches of rain. This led to street flooding along Highway 60 near Plant City, resulting in two vehicles ending up in the ditch and becoming partially submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed pictures of two vehicles in a flooded ditch along Highway 60 south of Plant City. The water was up to the hood of both cars. Nearby rain gauges reported as much as 7 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low over the NE Gulf of Mexico increased diurnal instability with enhanced a few storms that developed along the sea breezes as they drifted inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on the 2200 Block of SW 41st Avenue. The cost of the damage was unknown but it was estimated for the event to be included in StormData.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low across the NE Gulf of Mexico began to lift NE with a lobe of strong mid level energy overspreading the area from west to east. This energy enhanced thunderstorms that developed along the west coast sea breeze and east coast sea breeze mergers in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across International Golf Parkway about 1 mile west of U.S. Highway 1. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown but it was estimated for the inclusion of the event in StormData.

This energy enhanced thunderstorms that developed along the west coast sea breeze and east coast sea breeze mergers in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees limbs were blown down with wind estimated between 50-60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds blew down multiple trees and tore a section of a metal roof off with parts of the rafters attached from a pole barn in Codys Corner, near State Road 11 and County Road 304.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture combined with sea breeze forcing and diurnal heating produced localized heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer measured 2.31 inches of rainfall in 55 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture combined with sea breeze forcing and diurnal heating produced localized heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer measured 1.25 inches within 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low over the NE Gulf of Mexico increased diurnal instability with enhanced a few storms that developed along the sea breezes as they drifted inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree branch fell on a home in Ocala. Extent of damage unknown, but estimated cost of damage so event could be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore winds of 10-15 mph and surf of 1-2 feet with a south to north long-shore current were observed during the afternoon of the rip current fatality. St. Johns County Ocean Rescue reported that two others were caught in rip currents that day as well. The body of the fatality was bound about 400 yards north of where he entered the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ocean rescue and local media reported an 18 yr old male from Lowdes county GA drowned due to a rip current in St. Augustine Beach. The time of the fatality was unknown, but it occurred that afternoon per media reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary lifting northward over Florida under a mid level trough lingering across the eastern Gulf of Mexico resulted in waves of heavy rainfall, which produced daily rainfall amounts over 4 inches in southern Marion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Lake Weir measured 3.75 inches of rainfall between 5 am and 6:48 am. The 24-hr rainfall was 4.29 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking north over AL and northern GA streamed a plume of tropical moisture over the local area.  Waves of showers and thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a two hour period, 4.5 inches was measured in Mandarin. Ditches were overflowed with water, some spreading onto roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rain band formed across the area on the west side of TS Alberto. This band interacted with diurnal instability and upper level lift which combined to produce isolated severe storms as the band lifting NNE across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A former NWS employee estimated wind gusts of 70 mph near Lake Brooklyn.

A dry cold front pushed across the area in the morning trailed by gusty NW winds of 15-20 mph and minimum RHs falling to near 20%. A red flag warning was in effect. A wildfire developed in Nassau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire was near Yulee between State Road 200 and Pages Dairy Road. It burned about 8 acres and was 75% contained by 5:15 pm per local media. An evacuation order was lifted at 4:40 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed ahead of a cold front and moved south through the Nature Coast and Central Florida during the afternoon and early evening of the 7th. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported to be knocked down on Northwest 80th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed ahead of a cold front and moved south through the Nature Coast and Central Florida during the afternoon and early evening of the 7th. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 foot tree was uprooted by thunderstorm winds and fell across a road, landing on a carport. No other trees were knocked down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A teenager swimming along the beach was pulled under by a rip current while swimming with family and friends and later rescued. Numerous rip currents and rescues were reported along other Atlantic beaches during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 13 year old was swimming with family and friends near Coral Cove Park in Tequesta when he was reported to have been caught in a rip current and pulled under the surf. A good samaritan who witnessed the incident was able to pull the teenager from the water and begin CPR. The teenager was transported to a nearby hospital in critical condition. Report received via WPTV on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough and pre-frontal trough over a moist and unstable environment triggered severe thunderstorm that produced large hail and gusty winds. Strong deep layer shear and most unstable CAPEs where near 2500 J/kg with steep mid level lapse rates (over 8C/km). The Storm Prediction Center highlighted an Enhanced Risk of Severe Storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 1 hail was reported near Macclenny.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread hail up to ping-pong ball size was reported near Bryceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall line moved into the west coast of Florida ahead of a fast moving cold front. Some of the stronger storms produced severe wind gusts, and one waterspout came onshore as a brief lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Venice Municipal Airport (KVNC) recorded a 56 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

A sea breeze weather pattern across South Florida, where showers and thunderstorms developed along sea breeze and boundary collisions. Thunderstorms developed across the western east coast metro in the afternoon where a boundary collision allowed a funnel cloud to form over western Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple members of the broadcast media reported a funnel cloud near Markham Park area around 3PM EDT associated with an area of showers and thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area over Florida and moist southwest wind flow led to the development of showers and thunderstorms across much of South Florida during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted about halfway to the ground near US 1 about 2 miles south of Florida City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall line moved into the west coast of Florida ahead of a fast moving cold front. Some of the stronger storms produced severe wind gusts, and one waterspout came onshore as a brief lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow station at Egmont Key (XEGM) recorded a 51 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorm caused minor damage near Destin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph downed a few trees and power lines. Also damage to a roof and fence along Stahlman Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced lightning which damaged houses in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Pace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced lightning which damaged houses in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a residential building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple barns were damaged and one was completely destroyed. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated so the event could be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the first time since January 2017, an event including both flooding and tornadoes impacted our area starting on February 11, 2018. Two tornadoes touched down across inland portions of the Florida Panhandle before sunrise, followed by persistent heavy rainfall that resulted in flooding at several river sites.  As an upper level trough dug southward across the western and central U.S., southerly flow drew moisture from the Gulf of Mexico northward into the tri-state area over the weekend. Upper air soundings measured this increased moisture with precipitable water values around 1.5 inches on Sunday, well above the 90th percentile and a record for the 12z February 11 sounding, although the record for that date and time are below the average max for this time of year. With a cold front and upper level trough west of the area, the persistent deep layer moisture helped to create a favorable set-up for high rainfall amounts.  This wet pattern resulted in copious amounts of rainfall across the tri-state region. The highest rainfall amounts were across the Florida Panhandle where 8 to 10 inches fell with localized areas receiving more than 10 inches across coastal Walton and Bay counties. The highest observed rainfall total was in Walton County, where a 48-hour rainfall total of 11.45 inches was recorded. Across southeast Alabama and portions of southwest Georgia, widespread 6 to 8 inches of rain fell. While one Flash Flood Warning was issued, the majority of the impacts from this event were from moderate rainfall rates over a prolonged period of time as convection trained across the tri-state area. These high rainfall totals resulted in numerous road closures across the Florida Panhandle from water covered roads and washed out roadways. In addition, a few roads were closed across southeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered the roadway at Bunker and Steel Field Roads and resulted in road closure.  A small creek intersects this area and likely overflowed from a long duration of moderate to occasionally heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved east through the Tampa Bay area throughout the late morning and early afternoon hours. One storm developed a brief tornado as it interacted with an outflow boundary, damaging the roof of an apartment in Tampa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported on moderate roof damage to a 2 story apartment building, where a portion of a roof was removed. Little, if any, surrounding damage was observed. A weak radar rotation was observed in the vicinity at the same time, indicating that the damage was likely caused by a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and westerly winds allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop along the west coast of Florida during the early afternoon and push east through the afternoon and early evening. One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts. A lightning strike from another storm sparked a house fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a fire in the attic of a house in Lakeland. Fire crews arrived and quickly put out the fire before it spread to the rest of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along sea breeze boundaries and pushed west into the Nature Coast during the early evening. One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts in Citrus County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citrus County Emergency Management reported some tree limbs and small branches were knocked down. A sign mounted on a metal pipe was also knocked down, but the pipe was reported to be old and rusted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong surface heating led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms developing across the Florida Peninsula along sea breeze and outflow boundaries. One of these storms produced Nickel sized hail in southwest Florida.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across the Florida Peninsula during the afternoon and evening hours. A lightning strike from one of the storms caused a fire at an apartment building in Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media widely reported on a fire at a 3 story apartment complex that was believed to be started by a lightning strike. The fire caused major damage to the roof and top floor of the building, damaging 24 units and displacing around 100 residents. No injuries were reported from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and westerly winds allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop along the west coast of Florida during the early afternoon and push east through the afternoon and early evening. One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts. A lightning strike from another storm sparked a house fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were reported to have knocked down large tree limbs and to have damaged several awnings in Zephyrhills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man got caught in a rip current off Lido Key Beach and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man and a woman were swimming at Lido Key Beach after sunset when they were pulled out into deeper waters by a rip current. The woman swam against the current for an hour before reaching the shore. Rescue crews found the man's body, but were unable to revive him.

Snow and sleet fell across the tri-state area on Wednesday, January 17, 2018 with some areas in southeast Alabama receiving around an inch of snow and sleet. This was the second winter weather event for the tri-state area in two weeks! Unlike the previous event on January 3 where the snow/sleet/freezing rain impacted the Florida Big Bend and south central GA, the Wiregrass region of Southeast Alabama, Southwestern Georgia, and portions of the Florida Panhandle received the frozen precipitation during this event. Another key difference with this event that were the warm temperatures during the lead up to the event, which affected the breadth of storm impacts across affected areas. Bitter cold air was already in place several days in advance across the region leading up to the January 3rd event, yielding greater impacts on both road and elevated surfaces alike. Above average highs remained in place region-wide January 7-12, with highs near 70 degrees and overnight lows remaining above freezing. Highs in the 50s and 60s were common in the immediate days leading up to January 17th. This period of warm weather limited impacts to elevated surfaces, such as bridges and overpasses, across the western half of the area where the precipitation band and cold air were coupled. A warm nose above the surface across the Florida Big Bend and extreme southern Georgia prevented a changeover from rain to wintry precipitation, before dry air eroded the precipitation band away over the Suwanee Valley. Bitter cold set in across the entire region thereafter, subjecting people to dangerous wind chills and a hard freeze on the night of the 17th. Cold overnight lows persisted on the 18th before a quick warmup trend commenced for the end of the week and into the weekend.

Unlike the previous event on January 3 where the snow/sleet/freezing rain impacted the Florida Big Bend and south central GA, the Wiregrass region of Southeast Alabama, Southwestern Georgia, and portions of the Florida Panhandle received the frozen precipitation during this event. Another key difference with this event that were the warm temperatures during the lead up to the event, which affected the breadth of storm impacts across affected areas. Bitter cold air was already in place several days in advance across the region leading up to the January 3rd event, yielding greater impacts on both road and elevated surfaces alike. Above average highs remained in place region-wide January 7-12, with highs near 70 degrees and overnight lows remaining above freezing. Highs in the 50s and 60s were common in the immediate days leading up to January 17th. This period of warm weather limited impacts to elevated surfaces, such as bridges and overpasses, across the western half of the area where the precipitation band and cold air were coupled. A warm nose above the surface across the Florida Big Bend and extreme southern Georgia prevented a changeover from rain to wintry precipitation, before dry air eroded the precipitation band away over the Suwanee Valley. Bitter cold set in across the entire region thereafter, subjecting people to dangerous wind chills and a hard freeze on the night of the 17th. Cold overnight lows persisted on the 18th before a quick warmup trend commenced for the end of the week and into the weekend.

A strong cold front followed by an air mass of arctic origin allowed temperatures in Hendry County to drop to below freezing on the morning of January 18th. These temperatures combined with light winds overnight allowed frost to form on crops across farms in northern portions of the county. Tomato, squash, pepper and sugarcane crops were all affected by the cold weather outbreak and associated frost/freeze conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hendry County Association of Agricultural Agents reported damage to crops due to cold temperatures. Frost was able to develop and destroy some crops across the area. These included about 40 acres of squash, 55 acres of peppers and 19 acres of tomatoes. Sugarcane was also severely damaged when frost formed in the shoot of the sugarcane crop.||Temperatures dropped into upper 20s across most of this area, with duration of below freezing temperatures of about 3-4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep cold airmass was in place across the southeast region. An area of low pressure deepened near the Bahamas during the day as a vigorous upper level trough swept over the region from the west. The upper level lift, cold airmass, and strong low level winds created an area of freezing precipitation across the forecast area, with freezing rain up to 0.50 thick across NE FL and SE GA generally south of Waycross, with a historic snowfall from Homerville to Waycross to Jesup northward with amounts of 2-4 inches and up to 6 inches. Widespread power outages occurred due to frozen precipitation and numerous roads were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:18 am, the public reported freezing rain was starting to accumulate on vegetation and vehicles in Lake City. At 6 am, the broadcast media reported freezing rain in Lake City. At 9:30 am, the public reported an accumulation of 0.25 inches of freezing rain in Lake City. At 9:50 am, the media reported power outages in Lake City, as well as south of town. At 11:33 am, law enforcement reported freezing rain accumulation about 1 mile SE of Lake City caused power lines and tree limbs to fall down. At 1:41 pm, the public estimated a quarter inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain in Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cutoff upper-level low over the Florida panhandle and northeast Gulf of Mexico, and the Atlantic ridge axis over the Florida Straits and the northern Bahamas generated deep southwest flow across central Florida. Despite early day showers and storms, clouds cleared during the afternoon allowing daytime heating to occur. Both the east coast and west coast sea breezes developed, with the west coast being more progressive due to southwest winds. As the two collided over eastern Orange and Osceola counties, numerous thunderstorms formed along a line from Orlando south to Lake Okeechobee. As the line of storms pushed east into Brevard County, several reports of severe wind gusts were received from the Cape south across the intracoastal waters of central Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force Wind Tower 0509 measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph or 51 knots from the west-southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a deadly 4 day period of rip currents across coastal Alabama and the western Florida Panhandle. From August 1st to August 5th, six people were killed along the stretch of coast from Dauphin Island, AL to Destin, FL. 3 occurred in Alabama and 3 occurred in Florida. Numerous water rescues occurred, resulting in several people being transported to local hospitals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destin Beach safety reported a total of 86 rip current rescues were conducted on August 3rd with 5 patients transported to the local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a high wind gust near Eglin AFB.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Eglin AFB Weather ops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning of January 3rd, portions of north Florida and south Georgia had a mix of wintry precipitation. The precipitation was primarily freezing rain mixed with some sleet around Cross City and Mayo, Florida with ice accumulations estimated at a quarter to a half inch.  In the areas around Tallahassee, Florida, and Valdosta, Georgia, the precipitation was initially freezing rain and sleet, but changed to snow. The heaviest accumulations of snow were in south Georgia where amounts ranged from 0.50 to 2 inches. Snow accumulations in north Florida were mostly less than half an inch, with only 0.1 inches measured officially in Tallahassee.  For this region it was the first winter storm since December 1989.||Impacts were significant across the area.  The ice accumulations in the Suwannee River Valley were enough to cause numerous power outages, especially in Lafayette county.  Due to recent cold temperatures, ice also accumulated on numerous bridges and overpasses, resulting in a large section of I-10 being closed from Leon county eastward into the Suwannee Valley.  In Georgia, all bridges and overpasses in the city of Valdosta were closed due to icy conditions.  Around 105 accidents were reported in Lowndes county during the event, which is the most local officials could ever remember occurring in the county during one day.

Early in the morning of January 3rd, portions of north Florida and south Georgia had a mix of wintry precipitation. The precipitation was primarily freezing rain mixed with some sleet around Cross City and Mayo, Florida with ice accumulations estimated at a quarter to a half inch.  In the areas around Tallahassee, Florida, and Valdosta, Georgia, the precipitation was initially freezing rain and sleet, but changed to snow. The heaviest accumulations of snow were in south Georgia where amounts ranged from 0.50 to 2 inches. Snow accumulations in north Florida were mostly less than half an inch, with only 0.1 inches measured officially in Tallahassee.  For this region it was the first winter storm since December 1989.||Impacts were significant across the area.  The ice accumulations in the Suwannee River Valley were enough to cause numerous power outages, especially in Lafayette county.  Due to recent cold temperatures, ice also accumulated on numerous bridges and overpasses, resulting in a large section of I-10 being closed from Leon county eastward into the Suwannee Valley.  In Georgia, all bridges and overpasses in the city of Valdosta were closed due to icy conditions.  Around 105 accidents were reported in Lowndes county during the event, which is the most local officials could ever remember occurring in the county during one day.

It was a deadly 4 day period of rip currents across coastal Alabama and the western Florida Panhandle. From August 1st to August 5th, six people were killed along the stretch of coast from Dauphin Island, AL to Destin, FL. 3 occurred in Alabama and 3 occurred in Florida. Numerous water rescues occurred, resulting in several people being transported to local hospitals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 30 year old man was caught in a rip current near Opal Beach. He was pulled from the water by lifeguards and taken via helicopter to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18 year old female from Pearl, Mississippi got caught in a rip current and drowned near beach access #7 on Okaloosa Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved southeast through the Florida Peninsula on the 17th, with strong cold air advection causing a hard freeze over large portions of the Nature Coast and west central Florida during the morning of the 18th. Another hard freeze occurred on the morning of the 19th as light winds and clear skies allowed for strong radiational cooling.||The freeze caused unknown amounts of damage to citrus crops in Hernando, Pasco, Hillsborough, Polk, Manatee, Sarasota, Hardee, DeSoto, and Highlands Counties. There was also an unknown amount of damage to strawberry and crops in Hillsborough, Polk, Manatee, Sarasota, and Hardee County. In addition, the freeze also damaged landscaping across west central and southwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the mid to upper 20s across Polk County for several hours on the morning of the 18th, causing damage to citrus groves, strawberry crops, and landscaping. The coldest temperature reported was 25 degrees at the COOP sites in Lakeland and Lake Wales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure over GA combined with a low level trough in addition to sea breeze forcing triggered showers and isolated thunderstorms. Shear was about 15-25 kts, less than previous days, supported only isolated strong to pulse severe storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado touched down along Rio del Norte along the Intracoastal Waterway in the Palencia area. Minor damage occurred to tow newly constructed roofs and a fence was partially blown down. Several trees were blown down and uprooted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the first time since January 2017, an event including both flooding and tornadoes impacted our area starting on February 11, 2018. Two tornadoes touched down across inland portions of the Florida Panhandle before sunrise, followed by persistent heavy rainfall that resulted in flooding at several river sites.  As an upper level trough dug southward across the western and central U.S., southerly flow drew moisture from the Gulf of Mexico northward into the tri-state area over the weekend. Upper air soundings measured this increased moisture with precipitable water values around 1.5 inches on Sunday, well above the 90th percentile and a record for the 12z February 11 sounding, although the record for that date and time are below the average max for this time of year. With a cold front and upper level trough west of the area, the persistent deep layer moisture helped to create a favorable set-up for high rainfall amounts.  This wet pattern resulted in copious amounts of rainfall across the tri-state region. The highest rainfall amounts were across the Florida Panhandle where 8 to 10 inches fell with localized areas receiving more than 10 inches across coastal Walton and Bay counties. The highest observed rainfall total was in Walton County, where a 48-hour rainfall total of 11.45 inches was recorded. Across southeast Alabama and portions of southwest Georgia, widespread 6 to 8 inches of rain fell. While one Flash Flood Warning was issued, the majority of the impacts from this event were from moderate rainfall rates over a prolonged period of time as convection trained across the tri-state area. These high rainfall totals resulted in numerous road closures across the Florida Panhandle from water covered roads and washed out roadways. In addition, a few roads were closed across southeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over Andara Road caused a road closure between Ebenezer Road and Holmes Valley Road.  This was caused by a long duration of moderate to occasionally heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through South Florida, bringing large northerly swells, gusty winds and rough surf conditions. Seas along the Palm Beach County coast reached heights over 10 feet, causing dangerous beach conditions and the closure of Lake Worth and Juno Beach piers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 9-year-old girl was hit by a large wave while standing at the jetty at Jupiter Inlet and swept into the water.  She suffered a cut to the head, along with some scrapes and bruises but was expected to be OK.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through South Florida, bringing large northerly swells, gusty winds and rough surf conditions. Seas along the Palm Beach County coast reached heights over 10 feet, causing dangerous beach conditions and the closure of Lake Worth and Juno Beach piers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper in Palm Beach County reported two people sitting on the beach were pulled into the ocean by a large wave that crashed onshore. The two people were rescued to safety by a surfer and transported to a medical center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass with south southwesterly wind flow across South Florida ahead of a cold front. A line of thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front produced gusty winds in Marco Island in Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph / 53 knots was recorded by a Weatherbug mesonet site MRCSC located at the Charter Club of Marco Beach in Marco Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions combined with gusty winds allowed for wildfires to spread across Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked multiple wildfires across Collier County, with drier than normal conditions and gusty winds contributing to the rapid spread. Two of these fires the Greenway and 116th Avenue fire combined shortly after each ignited. The large fire burned 17,957 acres in interior Collier County, causing periodic road closures of State Road 29 and several other local roadways. A RV, hunting camp and another unknown structure were destroyed in the wildfire.

This tornado was rated EF0 with max winds estimated at 65 mph based on the observed damage.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cutoff upper-level low over the Florida panhandle and northeast Gulf of Mexico, and the Atlantic ridge axis over the Florida Straits and the northern Bahamas generated deep southwest flow across central Florida. Despite early day showers and storms, clouds cleared during the afternoon allowing daytime heating to occur. Both the east coast and west coast sea breezes developed, with the west coast being more progressive due to southwest winds. As the two collided over eastern Orange and Osceola counties, numerous thunderstorms formed along a line from Orlando south to Lake Okeechobee. As the line of storms pushed east into Brevard County, several reports of severe wind gusts were received from the Cape south across the intracoastal waters of central Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force Wind Tower 0311 measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph or 51 knots from the west-southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall from afternoon convection caused saturated grounds, with weakened root structure around a tree causing it to fall onto a mobile home. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An uprooted tree due to saturated ground fell on a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the first time since January 2017, an event including both flooding and tornadoes impacted our area starting on February 11, 2018. Two tornadoes touched down across inland portions of the Florida Panhandle before sunrise, followed by persistent heavy rainfall that resulted in flooding at several river sites.  As an upper level trough dug southward across the western and central U.S., southerly flow drew moisture from the Gulf of Mexico northward into the tri-state area over the weekend. Upper air soundings measured this increased moisture with precipitable water values around 1.5 inches on Sunday, well above the 90th percentile and a record for the 12z February 11 sounding, although the record for that date and time are below the average max for this time of year. With a cold front and upper level trough west of the area, the persistent deep layer moisture helped to create a favorable set-up for high rainfall amounts.  This wet pattern resulted in copious amounts of rainfall across the tri-state region. The highest rainfall amounts were across the Florida Panhandle where 8 to 10 inches fell with localized areas receiving more than 10 inches across coastal Walton and Bay counties. The highest observed rainfall total was in Walton County, where a 48-hour rainfall total of 11.45 inches was recorded. Across southeast Alabama and portions of southwest Georgia, widespread 6 to 8 inches of rain fell. While one Flash Flood Warning was issued, the majority of the impacts from this event were from moderate rainfall rates over a prolonged period of time as convection trained across the tri-state area. These high rainfall totals resulted in numerous road closures across the Florida Panhandle from water covered roads and washed out roadways. In addition, a few roads were closed across southeast Alabama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over the road at Dawkins Street caused a road closure from Sapp Road to Highway 79.  This was caused by a long duration of moderate to occasionally heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rotating wall cloud and funnel cloud were reported near Archer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main flow across central Florida was from the west-southwest due to a frontal trough moving across the mid-Atlantic states, and the axis of the Bermuda High Pressure situated across south Florida. Moisture was high across the area with Precipitable Water values around 1.80 to 2.00 inches, with temperatures aloft at 500 mb near -7 degrees Celsius. The atmosphere was favorable for showers and thunderstorms to develop along the east and west coast sea breezes. Scattered to numerous storms did develop across much of the area during the afternoon and evening. One storm became strong and produced penny-sized hail in Paisley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported penny-sized hail in Paisley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening mid-level trough and associated strong cold front moved across north/central Florida. Conditions ahead and along the cold front were favorable for severe thunderstorms as an 80-90 knot jet maxima produced sufficient upper level divergence. These conditions produced an enhanced risk of severe weather, sparking several rounds of severe thunderstorms, which affected Volusia, Seminole, Orange, Brevard and Okeechobee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A US Air Force wind tower measured a peak gust of 58 mph from the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening mid-level trough and associated strong cold front moved across north/central Florida. Conditions ahead and along the cold front were favorable for severe thunderstorms as an 80-90 knot jet maxima produced sufficient upper level divergence. These conditions produced an enhanced risk of severe weather, sparking several rounds of severe thunderstorms, which affected Volusia, Seminole, Orange, Brevard and Okeechobee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported in the Pine Hills area by a member of the public. Various other reports were received from the public, trained spotters, and emergency managers of hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size from Winter Garden/Ocoee to the Maitland/Eatonville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A TUTT was retrograding over Apalachee Bay with a moist and unstable airmass over the local area. Slow storm motion under an upper level trough axis created very weak storm motion, which promoted heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind and wave conditions in the local Atlantic lead to a moderate risk of rip currents along the Atlantic beaches on the day of April 2. A father and his children had to be rescued from a rip current that developed near Jupiter during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several swimmers got caught in rip currents at the 2000 block of South A1A in Jupiter. a father had to be rescued by Good Samaritans and fire rescue personnel after saving his own children. the father was taken to the hospital in critical condition while the other swimmers were ok. The father later passed away at the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moist south-southwesterly flow ahead of an approaching cold front lead to scattered shower and storm development along the east coast of South Florida during the afternoon hours. Interaction with the east coast seabreeze brought several strong to severe storms across Palm Beach and Broward Counties during the early to mid afternoon hours. Strong winds, two brief tornadoes, and small hail were reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey, in addition to videos received via social media, indicate that a weak tornado touched down in Downtown Fort Lauderdale near Andrews Avenue and NE 5th Street, knocking over construction fencing and barriers. The tornado then moved |southeast across Flagler Village, leading to minor tree damage in |the vicinity of NE 1st Avenue and NE 4th Street. The tornado |continued to move southeast, where video received via social media|shows the circulation crossing US 1 approximately near NE 1st |Street. ||The circulation then appears to have lifted, before touching down|south of the Tarpon River in the Rio Vista neighborhood. Several |streets with tree damage and minor debris, including trash cans, |were observed near SE 11th Avenue and SE 7th Street and Rio Vista |Boulevard and SE 10th Street. The tornado then likely crossed the |south fork of the Tarpon River, where the damage path becomes |diffuse as it moved across the SE 17th Street Causeway area and |into the north end of Port Everglades, possibly briefly as a |waterspout. Additional videos received via social media show that |the circulation then likely moved back onshore on the extreme |north end of the barrier island near Nova Southeastern University |Halmos College of Natural Sciences and Oceanography and then |quickly out into the Atlantic Ocean near the entrance to Port |Everglades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind and wave conditions in the local Atlantic lead to a moderate risk of rip currents along the Atlantic beaches on the day of April 2. A father and his children had to be rescued from a rip current that developed near Jupiter during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly couple was rescued from rip currents at the beach in Palm Beach Shores. They were taken to the hospital for exhaustion but were expected to be OK.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system combined with a moist and unstable air mass to generate strong and severe thunderstorms across the area. The storms produced several weak tornadoes along with wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved ashore on Okaloosa Island and produced signification damage to a home on Sailfish Dr. The tornado then moved across Santa Rosa sound and into Fort Walton Beach, producing tree damage and broken windows at St Mary Catholic Church and school. The tornado continued northeast across Robinwood Drive and damaged the roofs of several units in an apartment complex, before finally weakening and lifting just north of Jet Drive. Two minor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent dry weather during the late winter and early spring led to the development of severe drought conditions from northern and central Miami-Dade County through western Broward County to eastern Collier and far southwestern Palm Beach counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 to 50 percent of normal levels across a large part of coastal and metro Miami-Dade County, with a swath of less than 25% percent along the northern end of the county from North Miami Beach to Miami Lakes. This prolonged period of below normal rainfall led to low groundwater levels across this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent dry weather during the late winter and early spring led to the development of severe drought conditions from northern and central Miami-Dade County through western Broward County to eastern Collier and far southwestern Palm Beach counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 percent of normal levels over a large portion of the Everglades in Miami-Dade County, with a few spots near 10% of normal in the Water Conservation area 3. This prolonged period of below normal rainfall led to low groundwater levels across this area, including at Water Conservation area 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent dry weather during the late winter and early spring led to the development of severe drought conditions from northern and central Miami-Dade County through western Broward County to eastern Collier and far southwestern Palm Beach counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 percent of normal levels over a large portion of the Everglades and Big Cypress Reserve in eastern Collier County, with a few spots near 10% of normal in the far eastern portion of the county. Water levels at several reporting stations in the Big Cypress were at near historically low levels. ||These very dry conditions contributed to several lightning-triggered wildfires in the Big Cypress on April 10th, including the Vulture and Buzzard fires, and the Caracara fire reported on April 17th. These fires eventually became part of the Avian Complex which encompassed about 30,000 acres through the end of April.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 percent of normal levels over the Everglades of Broward and far southwestern Palm Beach counties, encompassing the Water Conservation Area 2 which was at very low levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very dry pattern over South Florida during the late winter and early spring eventually affected coastal sections of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions affecting inland portions of SW Florida eventually made to the the coastal sections of Collier County, where only 25 to 50% of the normal rainfall had occurred since February. This led to the spreading of severe drought conditions to the coastal sections of Collier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small shower moved onshore during the evening hours, producing a brief tornado over Ruskin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief and narrow damage track was found by an NWS storm survey crew, with scattered debris indicating an EF-0 tornado with a peak wind speed of 70 MPH.  The tornado formed west-southwest of the US Highway 41 bridge that crosses the Little Manatee River in Ruskin, FL. The tornado traveled east northeast and caused EF-0 damage to the Paradise Mobile Home Park. Wind speeds were based on mobile home and tree damage. Damage consisted of mobile home roof damage (including a collapsed car port) along with 4-5 inch snapped oak limb and fence damage. Light weight debris was thrown several hundred feet. Additionally, a shed and large construction dumpster were also moved several feet in a different direction to debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front was located just north of east central Florida. Cloud cover across the northern areas of east central Florida kept the atmosphere more stable and only scattered showers and a few storms developed. However, from Brevard County south through the Treasure Coast saw extensive daytime heating and this allowed for widespread showers and storms to develop along the many outflow boundaries present across the area. A severe thunderstorm developed in southern Brevard County where it dropped quarter-sized hail in Palm Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few social media reports were received from the Palm Bay area showing quarter-sized hail had occurred during a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Storm Prediction Center had designated a slight change of severe weather for all of east central Florida. A high amplitude shortwave over the Mississippi River Valley quickly moved south across the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and evening hours. A cold front associated with the intensifying trough was also observed crossing the Florida peninsula. Ahead of the cold front moderate thermodynamic instability with 700mb temperatures between -7 to -8 degrees Celsius, and 500mb between -9 to -10 degrees Celsius, resulting in lapse rates of 6-6.5 C/km. 90 knot winds atop a het streak over the north central Gulf of Mexico created resulting flow from the southwest. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front, eventually forming a line that crossed across all of east central Florida. Several thunderstorms became severe and produced wind damage in Orange and Volusia counties, as well as severe winds in Brevard and Indian River counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force wind tower 1007 measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph from the south.

Another Large tree was slit, falling on a house near 11th Avenue South and 43rd Street south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two foreign tourists were caught in a rip current near the Daytona Beach Pier around 4:30 pm local time. Life guards were able to rescue both swimmers, a 32-year-old male and a 26-year-old female. The male swimmer was unconscious, while the female swimmer was conscious after being rescued. Both were transported to a local hospital with injuries, where the male later died. The female swimmer was hospitalized and later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Smoke from a brush fire in Polk County was carried northward overnight into eastern Orange County. A localized area of dense fog set up near the intersection of State Road 520 and Nova Road in far eastern Orange County. Florida Highway Patrol reported two separate crashes involving 17 vehicles and resulting in 8 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized area of dense fog set up near the intersection of State Road 520 and Nova Road in far eastern Orange County. Florida Highway Patrol reported two separate crashes involving 17 vehicles and resulting in 8 injuries. The initial crash involved two vehicles. A car with a woman and infant child was rear ended as the woman began to slow down upon approaching the area of dense fog. The second wreck was a chain reaction to the first and involving 15 vehicles. Six injured victims were taken to Florida Hospital East. Two victims with minor injuries were taken to an Oviedo emergency room where they were later released. The infant in the first crash was later transferred to Arnold Palmer Hospital as a trauma alert patient.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level closed low at 500 mb across western Tennessee with a surface low pressure across northern Georgia caused the predominant southwest/west-southwest winds to veer to the west across east central Florida. A drier, cooler air mass moved in across the northern half of east central Florida through the afternoon hours, which led to greater instability across the Treasure Coast. Deep moisture moved eastward across southern Osceola and Okeechobee counties and the Treasure Coast resulting in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms from Okeechobee to St. Lucie to Maritn counties. One severe thunderstorm produced hail and strong winds in Hobe Sound, while a second severe thunderstorm produced golf ball sized hail in Tequesta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail in Hobe Sound.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon developed rapidly offshore Key Largo and Ocean Reef, then moved quickly northwest through the Upper Florida Keys.  Isolated tropical storm force wind gusts occurred at Ocean Reef and Key Largo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations at Key Largo and Ocean Reef recorded wind gusts peaking at 34 to 35 knots.  One report was received of a tree down partially onto one lane of County Road 905 (Card Sound Road), which runs between Key Largo and Ocean Reef, around 0541 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area.  This was an unusual, well organized severe weather event given the time of year.  The threat for some type of severe weather during the July 21-22 period was well advertised by the models a few days in advance, although the exact times of day that would be affected varied from run to run.  A special 18z KTAE sounding from July 22 revealed SBCAPE around 4500 j/kg with 0-6 km shear of 34 knots, which is unusually high for summer.  Some mid-level dry air also contributed to a favorable environment for damaging wind.  The end result was numerous reports of severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the southbound lane near W Capps Highway and St Augustine Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area.  This was an unusual, well organized severe weather event given the time of year.  The threat for some type of severe weather during the July 21-22 period was well advertised by the models a few days in advance, although the exact times of day that would be affected varied from run to run.  A special 18z KTAE sounding from July 22 revealed SBCAPE around 4500 j/kg with 0-6 km shear of 34 knots, which is unusually high for summer.  Some mid-level dry air also contributed to a favorable environment for damaging wind.  The end result was numerous reports of severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris blocked Highway 27 east of Highway 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms that developed along the seabreezes during the late morning hours across South Florida brought numerous boundary collisions into the early afternoon. One of these collisions lead to the development of a funnel cloud over southwestern Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of a funnel cloud was relayed to an off duty NWS meteorologist at 120 PM EDT. Photo shows it extending at least halfway to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continuing dry conditions brought favorable conditions for wildfire development and spread during the spring across South Florida. One of these wildfires in West Palm Beach lead to the brief evacuation of numerous homes and road closures, including a US highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports a 10 acre wildfire near the Whispering Oaks community in West Palm Beach. Approximately 50 homes were briefly evacuated as a precaution as well as a brief closure of US 98 in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moist south-southwesterly flow ahead of an approaching cold front lead to scattered shower and storm development along the east coast of South Florida during the afternoon hours. Interaction with the east coast seabreeze brought several strong to severe storms across Palm Beach and Broward Counties during the early to mid afternoon hours. Strong winds, two brief tornadoes, and small hail were reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph / 50 knots was recorded by mesonet weather station KFLOXAH26 in Loxahatchee Groves. The owner of the personal weather station also noted some small tree branches broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moist south-southwesterly flow ahead of an approaching cold front lead to scattered shower and storm development along the east coast of South Florida during the afternoon hours. Interaction with the east coast seabreeze brought several strong to severe storms across Palm Beach and Broward Counties during the early to mid afternoon hours. Strong winds, two brief tornadoes, and small hail were reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey summary: Multiple eyewitness videos show a tornado touched down along the northwestern side of Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport near I-595, leading to damage to cargo and storage containers in the FedEx facility on the north side of the  airfield. The tornado then moved east-southeast along the airport's northern taxiway, causing damage to a jet bridge on the north side of the terminal. The damage path becomes diffuse as it likely crossed Perimeter Road and US 1 along the immediate east side of the airport. The tornado then moved into the extreme |southern end of Port Everglades, where multiple cargo containers were flipped and damaged, along with eyewitness reports of port-a-johns being tossed about. The tornado then likely crossed the intracoastal waterway, and moved across the barrier island within Dr. Von D. Mizell-Eula Johnson State Port before moving offshore into the Atlantic Ocean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moist south-southwesterly flow ahead of an approaching cold front lead to scattered shower and storm development along the east coast of South Florida during the afternoon hours. Interaction with the east coast seabreeze brought several strong to severe storms across Palm Beach and Broward Counties during the early to mid afternoon hours. Strong winds, two brief tornadoes, and small hail were reported with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail along Interstate 595 near SW 66th Terrace. Several additional reports of pea to dime sized hail were received in Cooper City, Davie, and Plantation between 430pm and 445pm EDT. Wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph were recorded in the vicinity around the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and produced high winds and minor damage in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds snapped a cedar tree in the Point Baker area near Whiting Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon made landfall just west of the Alabama-Mississippi at 1015 PM CDT on September 4, 2018 with estimated maximum sustained winds of 70 mph. Gordon then tracked northwest into portions of inland southeast Mississippi late on the night of September 4th into the early morning of September 5th. ||Gordon produced sustained tropical storm force winds Greene and George counties in Mississippi, coastal Alabama, as well as Escambia County in Florida. The strongest winds were confined to the immediate coast, with Dauphin Island experiencing gusts to hurricane force in the northern eyewall. Wind gusts to hurricane force were reported on Dauphin Island with sustained tropical storm force winds reported as far north as the |Mobile Regional Airport. Some homes on Dauphin Island experienced roof and siding damage. Isolated to scattered down trees and power outages were reported in southern mobile county. Further east, tropical storm force winds, primarily in gusts, impacted areas of Baldwin county south of interstate 10 with the strongest winds along the immediate coast. Strong tropical storm force winds were felt on the Ft. Morgan peninsula where a few homes suffered some siding or roof damage. The steeple was blown off of the Romar Beach Baptist church in Orange Beach. Some trees were downed and scattered power outages were reported. In Escambia County, FL, tropical storm force wind gusts impacted the southern half of the county, especially in the Pensacola metro and the immediate coast. Sustained tropical storm force winds were reported at Pensacola NAS. One fatality was reported near Pensacola when a tree fell onto a mobile home and killed a small child. In Greene and George counties in Mississippi, tropical storm force winds downed trees and power lines, particularly in the Merrill and Richton communities. One home in Richton was significantly damaged by a downed tree. ||Storm surge inundation peaked between 2 to 3 feet across coastal Alabama and Escambia County, FL. This resulted in flooding of typically low lying areas of Mobile and Baldwin Counties, including the west end of Dauphin Island and the US Highway 90 Causeway. The Fort Pickens area in Florida experienced significant coastal flooding and beach erosion. ||Rainfall totals of 6-12 inches were recorded to the east of the center, primarily over Baldwin, Washington, Choctaw, Clarke, Escamba AL, Escambia FL, and Santa Rosa FL. The highest total measured was 12.45 inches 7 miles ENE of Gulf Breeze, FL. This resulted in areas of flash flooding, along with some significant river flooding. The Styx River reached major flood stage while the Fish River reached moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northbound and southbound US 29 ramps to Nine Mile road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow boundary moved south from the Florida Panhandle through the eastern Gulf of Mexico and onshore during the overnight and early morning hours of the 23rd. Numerous thunderstorms developed along the outflow, producing damaging wind gusts across west central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down on the road near State Road 54 and Rowan Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering stalled frontal boundary near Lake Okeechobee aided in the development of another day of scattered afternoon showers and storms across the region. Stronger storms that developed with boundary interactions across Palm Beach County produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported dime sized hail at the intersection of I-95 and Ocean Ave in Lantana. Additional reports of pea sized hail were received in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary lifting northward across the state and a cold front moving south down the peninsula brought numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon hours. As this activity interacted with the east coast seabreeze, several storms became severe, leading to a brief tornado and small hail in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported dime sized hail near Atlantic Boulevard and University Drive in Coral Springs. Wind gusts of around 50 mph were recorded in the vicinity around the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary lifting northward across the state and a cold front moving south down the peninsula brought numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon hours. As this activity interacted with the east coast seabreeze, several storms became severe, leading to a brief tornado and small hail in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down south of the Sawgrass Expressway near Country Hills Elementary School just east of Coral Springs Drive. The tornado then continued east-southeast along a discontinuous path, damaging the tops of trees and snapping and breaking off branches and other vegetation, as well as toppling power lines. The tornado damaged a street sign near the intersection of University Drive and Wiles Road, before lifting just south of Wiles Road near NW 85th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving pre-frontal squall line passing through the upper Florida Keys developed an embedded supercell thunderstorm with attendant mesocyclone along the Florida Bay side of Upper Matecumbe Key.  The supercell thunderstorm moved east-northeast, producing a waterspout which made landfall on the bayside of Plantation Key, Islamorada.  The tornado crossed the Overseas Highway, exiting oceanside just southwest of Tavernier Creek.  The waterspout then made a second landfall oceanside in Tavernier southeast of Tavernier Creek before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summary:  A waterspout or funnel cloud was observed backlit by |lightning over Florida Bay shortly before making landfall as a |tornado at Indian Mound Trail of the Indian Waterways subdivision of |Plantation Key, Islamorada. Large gumbo limbo tree limbs were downed |as well as the top of a palm tree, landing 20 yards from streetside |the front steps of a residence. And adjacent residence had a rolling |driveway gate completely dislodged from its mounts, pushed into a |trailered boat and car. The tornado continue to produce mostly |damage to large limbs of hardwood trees, including gumbo limbo and |mahogany, in a path rarely exceeded two residential properties in |width toward the east-northeast. An entire gutter and downspout |system was removed from a residence. Numerous loose items, including |filled trash cans and lawn/patio furniture was moved from the |backyards and understory of homes onto front lawns and streets. Most |of the observed damage in the Indian Waterways community were |consistent with 60 to 70 mph, with possible spot values near 75 mph.||The tornado crossed a patch of mangroves and undeveloped forest, and |reached greatest intensity at San Pedro Catholic Church. There, a |large tree split at the trunk, and an old-growth mahogany was |uprooted, falling toward the east onto a portion of the structure |connecting the rectory with the main church. Fortunately, the upper |portion of the tree did not substantially damage the roof, with no |leaks observed in the downpour that followed. However, several |barrel tiles were removed from the peak ridges forming the hip roof |pattern over the rectory, as well as from the southeast edge of the |church, onto the top of the church's main entrance. Numerous large |limbs were downed toward the east, with lightweight free-standing |signs deposited northward cross the tornado path from right to left.|Estimated wind speeds were maximized in the range of 90 to 100 mph |with maximum width near 60 yards. Good impact reference made to the |observed wind impacts at this location from recent Hurricane Irma, |all having previously been removed or repaired.  ||The tornado continued toward the east-northeast, crossing U.S. |Highway 1 and the Old Highway, but weakened such that only large |tree limbs were downed. From this point, the tornado likely moved |out over the oceanside as a waterspout into Hawk Channel, southwest |of the oceanside entrance to Tavernier Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving pre-frontal squall line passing through the upper Florida Keys developed an embedded supercell thunderstorm with attendant mesocyclone along the Florida Bay side of Upper Matecumbe Key.  The supercell thunderstorm moved east-northeast, producing a waterspout which made landfall on the bayside of Plantation Key, Islamorada.  The tornado crossed the Overseas Highway, exiting oceanside just southwest of Tavernier Creek.  The waterspout then made a second landfall oceanside in Tavernier southeast of Tavernier Creek before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second path, likely a continuation as the waterspout made landfall |near Lincoln Avenue on the far southwest portion of Tavernier's |oceanside. Progressive narrowing of the damage path down to nearly |30 yards as well as a turn toward the northwest or north-northwest |was observed.  Impacts lessened from near the waterfront, including |damaged fences and large limbs,gradually down to smaller limbs and |twigs was noted. No further impacts were observed northeast of Lowe |Street.  It is likely the wind speeds 55 to 65 mph were observed, |weakening to below 50 mph roughly 3 blocks from the waterfront.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area.  This was an unusual, well organized severe weather event given the time of year.  The threat for some type of severe weather during the July 21-22 period was well advertised by the models a few days in advance, although the exact times of day that would be affected varied from run to run.  A special 18z KTAE sounding from July 22 revealed SBCAPE around 4500 j/kg with 0-6 km shear of 34 knots, which is unusually high for summer.  Some mid-level dry air also contributed to a favorable environment for damaging wind.  The end result was numerous reports of severe weather across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SSW steering flow prevailed across the area with sea breeze storms producing gusty downburst winds due to dry mid level air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near 6209 NW 50th Terrace. The cost of damage was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gust front from a distant sea breeze triggered thunderstorm produced wind damage in Baker County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across the westbound Interstate 10 and caused a partial road closure. Ground was saturated from recent rainfall. The time of damage was based on radar data. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow combined with distant ENE swells from Hurricane Florence offshore of the Carolina coast brought a high risk of rip currents and increased water levels along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated water levels were reported at Jacksonville Beach with ocean waves pushing up tot the dune line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow combined with distant ENE swells from Hurricane Florence offshore of the Carolina coast brought a high risk of rip currents and increased water levels along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf was reported with breaker heights up to 7 ft at Jacksonville Beach and water up to the dune line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ridge axis of a surface low was located near the south shore of Lake Okeechobee. Also, an elongated southwest to northeast oriented upper-level trough was located across north Florida, while its mid-level reflection sat further south over central Florida. Throughout the day the surface ridge axis lifted slowly northward into central Florida. This pooled deep layer moisture across the area. The overall flow was light from the south to south-southwest, this combined with daytime heating allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop along local sea breezes, lake breezes, and residual outflow boundaries. One strong thunderstorm produced nickel-sized hail in Palm Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported dime to nickel sized hail in Palm Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined mid and upper level low pressure system was located just east of Florida. As these systems moved west over the state, they created enhanced lift and increased instability as cooler mid-level temperatures overspread the area. This provided a favorable environment for strong thunderstorms. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze and collision of outflow boundaries from early morning showers. One storm became strong over Tavares, where it produced a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on photographs and videos received from the public, local media, and emergency management, the NWS Melbourne determined a high-end EF-0 tornado, with winds of 80-85 mph,  occurred in Tavares. The damage showed a discontinuous tornado track moving to the southwest along a nearly one mile path near U.S. Highway 441. The tornado lasted approximately 5 minutes. The most significant damage occurred to a warehouse/mobile unit located behind a Winn Dixie near the 450th block of East Burleigh Boulevard in Tavares. This structure was destroyed, and a piece of sheet metal was lofted onto a nearby power pole. Also, the Fish Camp Lake Restaurant located at the intersection of U.S. Highway 441 and North Lakeshore Boulevard sustained moderate roof damage. The exterior kitchen exhaust fan was ripped off the roof and thrown onto a nearby street. Adjacent to the restaurant at the Lake Tire & Auto Store, several wood fences were knocked over and a small tree was uprooted, falling onto a trailer. Additional reports of very minor damage occurred along US Highway 441, such as small tree limbs and branches knocked off trees, and small fences knocked over. A member of the public relayed a video from a boat on Lake Eustis showing the tornado as it was impacting the area near the Fish Camp Lake Restaurant. DI8, DOD2, EXP-LB; DI3, DOD4, EXP-LB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low located off the Georgia coast was moving to the west-southwest across north Florida. Cool temperatures aloft combined with high Precipitable Water values, daytime heating, and sea breeze and outflow boundary interactions led to a high coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Initially thunderstorms developed along the east coast and west coast sea breezes, these storms then pushed inland from the coast. As the boundaries collided a thunderstorm developed over Orlando, where a severe wind gust was recorded just north of the Orlando International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Weather Underground mesonet site just north of the Orando International Airport measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnally enhanced sea breeze and a convergent band of convection extending across NE FL triggered a pulse severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto a home along Salt Marsh Circle in Ponte Vedra. Substantial damage occurred. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was unresponsive when he was pulled from the water by life guards, during a day with numerous rip current rescues. The man later died at a local hospital. The cause of death was drowning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1300LST a 61-year-old man from Michigan was unresponsive when life guards pulled him from the water near the 1700 block of A1A and North Shore Boulevard in Ormond-By-The-Sea. The life guards performed CPR on the man before he was transported to a local hospital, where he later died of drowning. According to Volusia County Ocean Rescue, over 140 people were rescued from rip currents in a two day period covering July 10-11. Strong rip currents were occurring due to large swells generated from Hurricane Chris located well to the north, offshore the eastern seaboard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough located over the Great Lakes and northeast states had a weak southward extension into the southeast states. This feature caused the the Atlantic high pressure ridge to sink into south Florida, allowing southwest flow to become established across central Florida. Moisture was abundant across the area with high Precipitable Water values. This large-scale pattern combined with daytime heating and cool temperatures aloft to cause numerous showers and thunderstorms development across the area. The strong southwest winds kept the east coast sea breezed pinned very close to the coast, and the strongest storms formed later in the day as the two sea breezes collided near the east coast. One thunderstorm became severe as it moved from northeast Osceola County into central Brevard County. There the storm produced several damaging wind impacts in Rockledge and Cocoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of wind related damage were received from Brevard County Emergency Management and Cocoa Police. Several trees were toppled in Cocoa, including near the intersection of Pinyon Drive and State Road 524, at the 1700 block of Lake Drive in Cocoa,  and across the southbound lane of Clear Lake Road near the Coca-Cola Bottling Company. In Rockledge, a tree fell onto a home near the corner of Medallion Drive and Fiske Boulevard, causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed, and another injured in Ormond Beach when winds from a non-severe thunderstorm knocked over a large, mostly dead tree onto them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from Ormond Beach police and multiple local broadcast media indicated two people were injured when a large tree fell onto them in Ormond Beach. Both men were unloading furniture from a truck in the driveway of a home near Arroyo Parkway when the tree fell, and were transported to a local hospital with injuries. One man suffered a critical head injury during the incident and died the following day (July 23).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused numerous trees to be snapped and uprooted in Ponce de Leon.  Minor structural damage was sustained as roofing was peeled off of a couple of metal barns.  Winds were estimated around 80 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon sea breeze thunderstorms developing along the Interstate 75 corridor, producing gusty winds in Hernando County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported a few trees down across potions of State Road 50 causing the road to be briefly closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combined forcing of a backdoor cold front sliding from NE to SW across the area and the more dominant east coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass triggered strong storms as localized heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 6:37 pm, local media reported street flooding in Springfield near West 3rd Street and Main Street. At 6:38 pm, media reported that the Hart Bridge ramp from East Duval Street was closed due to flooded roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combined forcing of a backdoor cold front sliding from NE to SW across the area and the more dominant east coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass triggered strong storms as localized heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree debris was blown down along University Blvd near Blue Cypress Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the Florida Peninsula under deep moisture. A lightning strike from one of these storms caused a fire at an office building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire was reported at an office building near Dade City that was suspected to have been caused by lightning. Broadcast media reported that there was nearly $50,000 in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed under easterly flow. One of these storms produced a downburst wind gust that caused damage along the Tampa Bay in Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down and some roofing was removed from one mobile home due to downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow boundary moved south from the Florida Panhandle through the eastern Gulf of Mexico and onshore during the overnight and early morning hours of the 23rd. Numerous thunderstorms developed along the outflow, producing damaging wind gusts across west central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant wind damage was found in the Carlton Inlet Drive area of Bradenton. Sections of roof decking and shingles were removed from an apartment building, trees and branches were knocked down, and gutters, fascia and porch covers were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong outflow boundary moved south from the Florida Panhandle through the eastern Gulf of Mexico and onshore during the overnight and early morning hours of the 23rd. Numerous thunderstorms developed along the outflow, producing damaging wind gusts across west central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind damage was reported at Coach House Mobile Home Park, with trees and power lines down and roof damage to mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area. Some of the storms were marginally severe with impacts to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six trees were blown down on a property off John Pitts Road in the Bayou George area.  This report was obtained via social media with video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area. Some of the storms were marginally severe with impacts to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in a pasture off Nadine Road.  One tree fell on a fence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fences and trees were blown down in the Bylsma Manor area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fence was partially blown over in the Forest Park area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in the Panama City area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported on Highway 231 north of Panama City.
