EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the eastern Gulf and moved onshore throughout the day. Lightning strikes from some of these storms damaged at least three buildings in Pinellas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire reported at a bank in Dunedin was suspected to be caused by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was rescued from rough surf at New Smyrna Beach and transported to a nearby hospital in critical condition where he later died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent swells of 3 to 4 feet produced rough, breaking surf at New Smyrna Beach, together with enhanced rip currents. A 43 year old man from Orlando was rescued from the surf and transported to a nearby hospital in critical condition where he later died. The man was surfing prior to being found unresponsive in the water. An autopsy confirmed the man died from drowning. Life guards attributed the fatality to the rough surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the eastern Gulf and moved onshore throughout the day. Lightning strikes from some of these storms damaged at least three buildings in Pinellas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire reported at a house in Palm Harbor that was suspected to be caused by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the eastern Gulf and moved onshore throughout the day. Lightning strikes from some of these storms damaged at least three buildings in Pinellas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit a home in Palm Harbor, leaving a burn mark in a bathroom ceiling. Sparks from the strike also caused minor burns to a resident laying in a bed in the home. No fire was caused by the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon sea breeze thunderstorms across southwest Florida produced sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed a report from the public of penny size hail near McGregor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents and rough surf associated with distant Hurricane Florence affected the east-central Florida beaches for several days. Four people were pulled seaward by a strong rip current and one of them drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people were pulled seaward within a strong rip current at New Smyrna Beach. Life Guards rescued them, but one person was floating unconscious and unresponsive when contact was made. Fire Rescue personnel performed life-saving efforts on the 64-year old man from the nearby town of Edgewater before he was taken to a hospital, where he died. The incident occurred in unguarded water about 300 yards south of an open life guard tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of pulse severe convection for this warm season occurred today with scattered storms developing in a warm and unstable airmass favorable for downbursts.  A few impacts to trees and power lines occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down onto SR-267.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of pulse severe convection for this warm season occurred today with scattered storms developing in a warm and unstable airmass favorable for downbursts.  A few impacts to trees and power lines occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Shadeville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of pulse severe convection for this warm season occurred today with scattered storms developing in a warm and unstable airmass favorable for downbursts.  A few impacts to trees and power lines occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across Apalachee Parkway near Harrison Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first day of pulse severe convection for this warm season occurred today with scattered storms developing in a warm and unstable airmass favorable for downbursts.  A few impacts to trees and power lines occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto power lines on Williams Road just south of Apalachee Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 61 year old male died while snorkeling off of Panama City Beach in the water behind Regency Towers, 5801 Thomas Drive.  Double red flags were flying.

A slow moving area of thunderstorms across portions of southwest Wakulla county dumped 6-10 inches of rain in just a few hours during the early morning of May 21st.  Some flash flooding occurred near Sopchoppy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sopchoppy post office reported that several side roads off of Highway 319 were flooded south of Sopchoppy.  Between 6 and 10 inches of rain fell in just a few hours in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical Storm Alberto formed in the northwestern Caribbean Sea on the morning of May 25th and moved north through through the eastern Gulf before making landfall near Laguna Beach Florida on the afternoon of the 28th with a minimum central pressure of 990mb. The highest wind gusts in west central Florida was 41 MPH in Pinellas and Highlands counties. Storm surge was confined to no more than 2 feet over Levy and Citrus counties, with minimal flooding and beach erosion impacts. The storm total rainfall across the area was mostly around 1 to 2 inches, although isolated areas saw higher amounts with the highest reported total being 6.60 inches at Dover in Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Subtropical Storm Alberto formed in the northwestern Caribbean Sea on the morning of May 25th and moved north through through the eastern Gulf before making landfall near Laguna Beach Florida on the afternoon of the 28th with a minimum central pressure of 990mb. Storm surge was confined to no more than 2 feet over Levy and Citrus counties, with minimal flooding and beach erosion impacts. The storm total rainfall across the area was mostly around 1 to 2 inches, although isolated areas saw higher amounts with the highest reported total being 6.60 inches at Dover in Hillsborough County.||A mesonet station near Archbold reported a tropical storm wind gust of 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure extending from that Atlantic into the Carolinas, setup light southeast flow across the southwest Florida coastline. One man drowned after being caught in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 year old man was visiting Siesta Key from Missouri when he was pulled away from the coast by a rip current. The man was pulled back to the beach and was given CPR, but died from his injuries. Time of the drowning is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf associated with distant Hurricane Florence produced several days of high surf along the Florida east coast. Breaking surf peaked at 6-9 feet on September 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life guards rescued over a dozen people from the dangerous surf, primarily on Sunday, September 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf associated with distant Hurricane Florence produced several days of high surf along the Florida east coast. Breaking surf peaked at 6-9 feet on September 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life guards rescued numerous people from the dangerous surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late afternoon thunderstorm across eastern Hillsborough county produced a small area of penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Fish Hawk reported penny size hail falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical Storm Alberto affected portions of the Florida panhandle and southeast Alabama on May 28th.  County by county impacts are as follows.  ||In Walton county, construction barrels were blown onto Highway 98 in Miramar Beach.  A tree fell and knocked down power lines across the road at 186 Fine Hill Dr in De Funiak Springs.  Trees were blown down at Spence Dr and Highway 81, at Oak Grove Road and County Road 181E, and at South Jackson Street.  Flooding was reported in Santa Rosa Beach near the intersection of County Highway 395 and East Point Washington Road and along Flounder Street and Dolphin Way.  Inlet Beach Park beach access road flooded on Lakeshore Drive.||In Taylor county, the Steinhatchee boat ramp flooded with some minor overwash into the adjacent parking lot.  On the Aucilla River, the boat ramp at Mandalay Bay was washed off pilings onto dry line.||In Wakulla county, there were approximately 40 trees and power lines blown down and  a few low lying roads flooded.  There was one report of damage to a structure from a tree falling.  For the St. Mark River at Highway 98, there was 1.5 feet of inundation at the Newport boat ramp.  In the town of St. Marks, Riverside Drive was flooded up to a 1 foot depth and inundation extended from Riverfront up to 1000 ft inland northward along Port Leon Drive.  Water entered a restaurant.  Old Fort Road and areas near the fort flooded with peak inundations with 2.5 ft.  There was minor erosion at Shell Point Beach.  There were five homes with water underneath them on Beatty Taff Drive.  Mashes Sand Beach from the beach front westward to Blue Crab Road was flooded.  Peak inundation near the beach front was 2.5 ft.  Inundation covered up to 3000 ft along the road.  Two homes at ground level had water reach an exterior wall, but no water entered the structures.||In Franklin county, Alligator Point Road was overwashed during the peak storm surge.  In Eastpoint, flooding affected the east end of the causeway to Apalachicola, Buck Street, the beach near Reed Court, and water was up to the rock at 10th Street and Highway 98.  In Apalachicola, the marina and Water Street flooded.  The beaches were also underwater at the east end of the St. George Island Plantation. ||In Coffee county, trees were down County Roads 321 and 342 near Elba, Highway 87 near Fuller Brothers, Highway 87 at MM 34, Highway 189 near Perry Store, County Road 153 near Tabernacle, and County Road 248 near Weeks Bridge in New Brockton.  Power lines were down on Lee Street in New Brockton, Elba near Weeks Tire, and in the downtown areas of Elba and Enterprise.  ||In Houston County, there were a few downed trees and a short power outage.  One tree partially blocked Brannon Stand Road on the west side of Dothan.||In Holmes county, there were a couple of reports of trees down blocking roadways at Carter Mack and Old Mill.  An oak tree also fell onto a mobile home.||In Jackson county, around eight trees were blown down with a few small power outages.||In Liberty county, there were small and sporadic power outages.||In Leon county, around 9000 homes lost power in Tallahassee due to downed trees and limbs.  ||In Jefferson county, a tree fell onto a car on I-10, resulting in two serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Flounder Street and Dolphin Way.

There were five homes with water underneath them on Beatty Taff Drive.  Mashes Sand Beach from the beach front westward to Blue Crab Road was flooded.  Peak inundation near the beach front was 2.5 ft.  Inundation covered up to 3000 ft along the road.  Two homes at ground level had water reach an exterior wall, but no water entered the structures.||In Franklin county, Alligator Point Road was overwashed during the peak storm surge.  In Eastpoint, flooding affected the east end of the causeway to Apalachicola, Buck Street, the beach near Reed Court, and water was up to the rock at 10th Street and Highway 98.  In Apalachicola, the marina and Water Street flooded.  The beaches were also underwater at the east end of the St. George Island Plantation. ||In Coffee county, trees were down County Roads 321 and 342 near Elba, Highway 87 near Fuller Brothers, Highway 87 at MM 34, Highway 189 near Perry Store, County Road 153 near Tabernacle, and County Road 248 near Weeks Bridge in New Brockton.  Power lines were down on Lee Street in New Brockton, Elba near Weeks Tire, and in the downtown areas of Elba and Enterprise.  ||In Houston County, there were a few downed trees and a short power outage. A 5 year old boy from Alabama died after getting caught up in rough surf at Panama City Beach during the evening of May 19th.  Panama City Beach fire rescue crews and a bystander performed CPR on the boy, but he died a short time later at a hospital.  Single red flags were flying.

A 31 year old male drowned, likely due to rip currents, near the Tropic Winds Condos in the 17000 block of Front Beach Road in the Panama City Beach area.  He was face down in the water when friends located him.  Single red flags were flying.||In addition, a 28 year old male died near the 700 block of Scenic Gulf Drive along the Walton county coast.  The man was swimming in the evening after lifeguards had left the beach.  Red flags were flying.

Showers and thunderstorms over the Gulf waters moved ashore overnight along the Gulf coast. One of these thunderstorms produced gusty winds causing damage to a local golf course along with tree and sign damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree branch damage was reported by Naples Daily News at Eagle Key Circle in East Naples. The Lakewood Country Club also received minor tree damage, street signs were down and a plastic garden shed was blown across the street. Radar estimated wind was around 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell since February continued through the middle of May, leading to lingering severe drought conditions over interior portions of South Florida. The dry conditions coupled with near-record low groundwater levels contributed to the spread of wildfires, including the Avian Complex in eastern Collier County which burned over 82,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 percent of normal levels over a large portion of the Everglades and Big Cypress Reserve in eastern Collier County, with a few spots near 10% of normal in the far eastern portion of the county. Water levels at several reporting stations in the Big Cypress were at near historically low levels. The Avian wildfire complex in the Big Cypress Preserve reached up to 82,400 acres before the onset of the rainy season in mid-May put an end to the severe drought by May 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged dry spell since February continued through the middle of May, leading to lingering severe drought conditions over parts of Miami-Dade County as well as western Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals since February were only at 25 to 50 percent of normal levels across a large part of coastal and metro Miami-Dade County, with a swath of less than 25% percent along the northern end of the county from North Miami Beach to Miami Lakes. This prolonged period of below normal rainfall led to low groundwater levels across this area. The onset of heavy rainfall and localized flooding associated with the rainy season put a quick end to the severe drought by May 15th.

Only 25 to 50% of the normal rainfall had occurred since February, leading to very low groundwater levels. The onset of heavy rainfall associated with the rainy season put a quick end to the severe drought by May 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon showers and storms developed along the sea breezes along each coast. The Gulf coast sea breeze produced a shower that developed a funnel cloud over Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud photo and video were reported via social media associated with a shower moving through the Naples area. The funnel did not touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the eastern Gulf of Mexico allowed brought abundant tropical moisture to the region. With all of this moisture scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along sea breeze and outflow boundaries. A few of these storms became severe producing  hail and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported just South of Flamingo Road. Other reports in the area of penny and nickel size hail between 545 and 555 PM EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A EF-0 tornado affected the community of The Acreage during the pre-dawn hours on May 14th. Damage consisted primary of snapped tree branches, uprooted trees and downed fences and power lines along the tornado's path. Several homes suffered minor roof damage in the form of shingles being blown off. Two homes sustained damage to side doors, and one home had a set of window panes blown out. Several sheds were damaged, a few horse stables lost their covering, and a chicken coop was destroyed. Estimated peak wind was in the 75-80 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant tropical moisture across the region associated with an upper level low pressure system allowed for widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop. One of these storms produced deadly lightning at a farm in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53-year-old woman was killed when struck by a lightning strike while working at C.W. Hendrix Farms in Parkland. Two other people in the immediate area were also struck and taken to nearby hospitals.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple media reports of flooding of a neighborhood in Davie. Heavy rainfall caused a canal to overflow into adjacent streets near SW 133rd Avenue and SW 5th Court in Davie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along a convergence ridge axis with a tropical moisture plume over the region allowed showers and thunderstorms to train over the same region producing flooding in Broward County. The higher than normal precipitable water values with north to south wind flow allowed storms to continue to re-develop over the same areas. Hazardous seas and surf zone conditions led to a rip current rescue in Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall led to flooded roadways in Dania Beach and Hollywood. A total of 5 to 7 inches of rain fell across the area, with as much as 4 inches in an hour. Areas of standing water were two to three feet deep on some local roadways. This led to roadways becoming impassable and local law enforcement closing roads along US-1 and near Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport, where the airfield had approximately 2 feet of standing water.

Hazardous seas and surf zone conditions led to a rip current rescue in Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across the region in the range of 6 to 8 inches of rain in 6 hours fell across the region. The heavy rainfall led canals and to overflow. Water pumps to drain canals were already at capacity. The overflowing canals led to flooding of roadways with some roadways in the region becoming impassable. Water entered a few structures, mainly caused by the wake left behind by passing vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rip current pulled a women out into the ocean just before 8 p.m. in the waters south of Boca Raton���s South Inlet Park, between the Palm Beach and Broward County lines. A man swam out to save her, but got caught in the rip current himself. Crews from Ocean Rescue, Boca Raton Fire Rescue and Broward County Fire Rescue helped save the swimmer. The woman was taken to a Broward County hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Gulf of Mexico and high pressure over the Atlantic pumped plenty of moisture in South Florida. This allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region. Some low-level convergence with modest helicity levels were enough to spin up a tornado and produce a funnel cloud over the east coast metro regions of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Member of Broward County Emergency Management reported a funnel cloud northwest of the Sawgrass Expressway around 3 PM EDT associated with a thunderstorm over the Everglades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents and rough surf associated with distant Hurricane Florence affected the east-central Florida beaches for several days. A boy and older male were playing in the surf when they were pulled seaward by a strong rip current and the older male drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 year old boy and 24 year old male from Orlando were playing in rough surf at Playalinda Beach in Brevard County. A strong rip current, enhanced by swell from distant Hurricane Florence pulled the two individuals seaward and the 24 year old disappeared under the surf. The boy's father and the girlfriend of the young man rushed into the water, where they found the man unresponsive. A surfer and paddleboarder helped bring everyone to shore. The man died from drowning.

Activity that developed along the east coast seabreeze in Miami-Dade County early in the afternoon produced numerous reports of hail over the western suburbs of Miami metro area from Doral to West Kendall. Activity that developed along the east coast seabreeze in Miami-Dade County early in the afternoon produced numerous reports of hail over the western suburbs of Miami metro area from Doral to West Kendall. Winds were recorded in the 40-50 mph range. Additional storms across Palm Beach County later in the afternoon and evening produced wind damage around Lake Okeechobee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public picture and video received of pea sized hail falling with embedded larger pieces estimated up to half ���dollar size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon showers and storms developing along the east coast seabreeze produced a lightning strike that destroyed a tree in Broward County. Gusty winds from these thunderstorms across other parts of Southeast Florida caused isolated, minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike destroyed a tree at a residence on the corner of SW 36th Court and SW 165th Avenue in Miramar. There was no other damage reported from the strike. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow and deep moisture produced scattered afternoon thunderstorms across the Nature Coast. A lightning strike from one of these storms killed a woman in Levy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 49 year old woman was stripping bark off a tree in her yard when the tree was struck by lightning, injuring her. She was transported to a hospital where she died the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving across the tropical Atlantic into the Bahamas formed into Tropical Storm Gordon near the South Florida peninsula. Although the effects from Tropical Storm Gordon were fairly minor for South Florida, sustained Tropical Storm force winds were felt across portions of Miami-Dade and Broward County. Miami-Dade and Broward reported slightly higher amount of power outages than a typical day across the region. Due to some heavy rainbands Miami-Dade county experienced some street flooding that led to partial lane closures. However, no structural flooding was reported with all flood impacts relatively minor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained tropical storm force winds of 34 knots was recorded by ASOS site KOPF located at Opa-Locka Airport. A wind gust of 44 knots was also recorded at the site at 813 AM EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported dime sized hail on 99th Avenue in Coral Springs. Multiple other reports of smaller hail in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: NE flow focused stronger east coast sea breeze inland with strong storms impacting Marion county into the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rain in a short period of time leading to localized flooding in the Pine Hills area of Orlando. A few roads were briefly closed to traffic after several cars become stranded due to high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced three to five inches of rain in less than two hours over the Pine Hills area of Orlando. A few major roadways were temporarily closed to traffic after three or four cars become stuck in water two to three feet deep within a very localized area. Poor or blocked drainage may have also contributed to the localized problem. The nearest rain gauge, located eight miles southeast at Orlando Executive Airport measured 3.83 inches in less than 3-hours and radar estimated rain at the flood location indicated four to five inches fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful low level moisture, the presence of dry air aloft and a westerly steering component caused scattered thunderstorms to develop over the western peninsula and move into the interior during the late afternoon. Several of the storms became severe, producing winds of 60 mph winds at Orlando International Airport, over the Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge and near Port Mayaca. A funnel cloud was also spotted near Tradition in St. Lucie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm become severe just west of Orlando International Airport then moved over the airfield. Downed powerlines were reported by a trained spotter, approximately 3 miles west of the airfield. The airport ASOS (KMCO) measured peak gusts of 60 mph at 1551LST and then 69 mph at 1554LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred across the area. As is typical with summer storms in this part of the country, some of the storms were marginally severe with impacts to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down and resulted in a fire along Gillman Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near the intersection of County Road 349 north and 208th Street. The time of damage was based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: USAF wind tower 0714 within the Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge, east of Titusville, recorded a peak wind of 58 mph from the southwest as a severe thunderstorm moved across a non-populated area of northern Merritt Island toward the Atlantic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful low level moisture, the presence of dry air aloft and a westerly steering component caused scattered thunderstorms to develop over the western peninsula and move into the interior during the late afternoon. A delayed public report confirmed numerous trees were downed between Port Mayaca and the Martin/Palm Beach County line. Another report received via social media revealed powerlines down in the same area on the eastern shore of Lake Okeechobee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful low level moisture, the presence of dry air aloft and a westerly steering component caused scattered thunderstorms to develop over the western peninsula and move into the interior during the late afternoon. Several of the storms became severe, producing winds of 60 mph winds at Orlando International Airport, over the Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge and near Port Mayaca. A funnel cloud was also spotted near Tradition in St. Lucie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Amateur Radio operator reported a brief rope-shaped funnel cloud descending approximately 85 percent of the way down from the storm. The funnel cloud was observed to the northwest as he was driving north on Interstate 95 near Port St. Lucie. Weather radar indicated that the funnel cloud was associated with a strong thunderstorm over western St. Lucie County, south of Okeechobee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located from the northeast Gulf of Mexico into the Carolinas, while an area of high pressure was located over the western Atlantic Ocean. This led to southwest winds across the Florida peninsula. Moisture was plentiful across the area and mid level temperatures were marginally cool. The combination of these factors set the stage for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across central Florida, with some storms becoming strong. The southwest winds favored the west coast sea breeze. By mid afternoon, scattered to numerous showers were developing across the Orlando Metro area, as the west and east coast sea breezes collided. One thunderstorm became strong as it moved across central Seminole County and produced wind damage to a home in Sanford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures relayed via social media showed a single large pool enclosure blown over onto the roof of the attached house in Sanford, along with damage to a wood fence. No additional damage was noted to the immediate vicinity and no other reports of damage were received from the surrounding area. Winds were estimated near 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze collisions produced thunderstorms with strong wind gusts across Orange and Seminole Counties during the early evening. One of the storms become briefly severe, resulting in wind damage to trees in the Metrowest portion of Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe and downed trees near the 3400 block of South Hiawassee Road in the Metrowest portion of southwest Orlando. Additional minor tree damage occurred nearby within Metrowest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed in association with an isolated thunderstorm which quickly formed just north of Indiantown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received of a funnel cloud to the north of Indiantown, in association with an isolated thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was standing in a Denny's parking lot talking with a coworker when she was struck by lightning. The lightning knocked her unconscious and she was in serious condition when taken to a local hospital. An eyewitness reported that it was hardly raining and that the strike came out of nowhere.

This rain on top of saturated soils resulted in minor flooding across the area in the form of areas of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Decaying showers and thunderstorms moved southeast from north Florida into central Florida throughout the day. Mid-level southwest flow across the Florida peninsula allowed deep moisture to move in from the eastern Gulf of Mexico. As storms moved into east central Florida, they encountered a more unstable atmosphere due to less cloud cover and daytime heating. As storms strengthened one became severe and produced a severe wind gust at the Cape in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force Wind Tower 0006 measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 38 year old male went into the water to rescue his parents, who had been swept out by a rip current.  His parents made it back in, but the son drowned.  His body was recovered on a personal watercraft.  Resuscitation by EMS and first responders was attempted with no success.  Beach flags for the area were red, but there are no flags in the vicinity of the drowning.
Several waves of heavy rainfall caused persistent river flooding along the Alapaha and Suwannee Rivers which significantly impacted interests in Hamilton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 12/5, the Hamilton EM reported extensive river flooding along the Alapaha and Little Alapaha rivers. In the Fox Hollow neighborhood, 4 homes were cut off from road access due to river flooding. Another 10-15 homes are expected to be cut off from access in the next 24 hrs in the 34th Loop Anderson's subdivision. Shelters were opened at the Field House and Old High School. On 12/6, the EM reported SW 86h Blvd and NW 79th Drive were closed due to flooding over roadways. On 12/11, water levels crested in the rivers. On 12/13 road closures continued in the county which included: SW 86th Blvd, NW 79th Drive, NW 23rd Blvd and County Road 143; from the intersection of NW 24th Ave and NW 45th Street all through the Florida Camp sites; NW 34th Street and Withlacoochee Walk Camp sites; NE 80th Blvd was closed from County Road 6 east toward NE 36th Drive; SW 74th Way (Suuwannee River Camp Sites). All boat ramps in the county were closed due to flooding.

A surface and 850 mb high pressure ridge axis was located well south of central Florida, which resulted in west low-level flow across the area. An upper-level ridge remained over the central Florida peninsula, although the axis had moved south to the Lake Okeechobee area. This setup favored the west coast sea breeze and most of the storms that developed to move towards the east coast. Scattered to numerous showers and storms did develop in the afternoon and early evening. One storm moved across the intracoastal waters of northern Brevard County where it produced severe winds. Also, one person was struck by lightning in Viera.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per reports relayed by local broadcast media: according to the Brevard County Sheriffs Office, one person was struck by lightning on Murrell Road in Viera. The victim was transported to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main flow across central Florida was from the west-southwest due to a frontal trough moving across the mid-Atlantic states, and the axis of the Bermuda High Pressure situated across south Florida. Moisture was high across the area with Precipitable Water values around 1.80 to 2.00 inches. Temperatures aloft at 500 mb were around -6 to -7 degrees Celsius. The atmosphere was favorable for showers and thunderstorms to develop along the east and west coast sea breezes. Due to the stronger west winds, the west coast sea breeze was dominant, and the main collision between the two sea breezes occurred closer to the east central Florida coast. Still, numerous showers and thunderstorms impacted much of east central Florida. One man was killed after being struck by lightning in Umatilla, while another man was injured after being struck by lightning in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per a report received via a local newspaper, a 44-year-old man was killed after being struck by lightning in Umatilla. At 1721 LST, a man was found unconscious by his neighbors on a small pontoon boat at his home on Lake Owen, on the 40000th block of Long Island Drive in Umatilla. The neighbors called the Lake County Sheriffs Office, and first responders transported the man to a local hospital where he was pronounced deceased. The medical examiners office determined the cause of death to be electrocution, and this is further supported by burn marks found on the seat and floor of the boat. NWS Melbourne meteorologists determined the lightning strike occurred around 1258 LST, this was confirmed by reports from the neighbors who heard loud bang of the strike many hours before finding the deceased man on his boat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The surface high pressure ridge was located across south Florida, which resulted in south-southwesterly winds across east central Florida. Moisture was abundant with Precipitable Water values around 2 inches. An initial thunderstorm developed in central Brevard County and moved over Rockledge, producing rain for approximately 15 minutes. A short time later, a stronger, slow-moving thunderstorm developed right over Rockledge and remain over the same area for around an hour and a half. In total, 5 to 6 inches of rain fell in a 1.5 by 1.5 square mile area in southwest Rockledge. Temporary, but considerable roadway flood resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 5 to 6 inches of rain in the Rockledge area were received from trained spotters and the public. Most of the rain fell between 1230 and 1430 LST, with light rain lingering for several more hours. The main areas impacted were between Interstate 95, Fiske Boulevard, Eyster Boulevard, Murrell Road and Roy Wall Boulevard. Street flooding was reported on Levitt Parkway and in the nearby Silver Pines Estates neighborhood.

The good samaritan helped revive one victim by performing life-saving CPR. Once firefighters arrived they provided medical assistance to the victims, and transported all of them to a local hospital where they were treated for minor injuries.

Reports of minor roof damage was received from local broadcast media, and a first-hand account from a member of the public who witnessed the tornado. A surface trough was moving south near the east Florida coast while a surface high pressure was situated near the Carolina coastline. The main steering flow winds were out of the north-northwest, although low-level winds were out of the east-southeast. Temperatures aloft were around -7 C to -8 C, and a deep plume of moisture with Precipitable Water values around 1.50 to 1.60 inches was situated over central Florida. The east coast sea breeze was dominant and progressed inland faster than the west coast sea breeze. As the two sea breezes collided in western Orange County, a thunderstorm quickly developed and became severe. This storm produced several reports of wind damage in Windermere and Ocoee in western Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received via social media of wind related damage in western Orange County. The first report was of a large Sycamore tree downed by winds near the West Oaks Mall in Ocoee. About five minutes later, reports were received of large trees downed and a wooden fence knocked over in Windermere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central Florida was wedged between a high pressure ridge axis to the south, and a weak low pressure trough to the north. This resulted in strong west flow in the low-levels across east central Florida, and allowed deep moisture to remain in place across the area with Precipitable Water values near 2 inches. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly along the quickly progressing west coast sea breeze. The strong west winds delayed the development of the east coast sea breeze by a few hours, but by mid-afternoon the sea breeze had developed in Brevard County. Showers and thunderstorms began to develop along the slowly advancing sea breeze. Further south, in Indian River County, a thunderstorm there produced a northward moving outflow boundary that pushed into Brevard County and interacted with the sea breeze. This caused enhanced lift just along the sea breeze over Melbourne, and a severe thunderstorm quickly developed. This storm produced a severe wind gust in Melbourne Beach. In Port Orange, a home was destroyed after being struck by lightning and catching fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: APRS site DW8248 measured a peak wind gust of 50 knots or 58 mph from the northwest. Around 10 minutes later the same site gusted to 53 knots or 61 mph..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the Florida Peninsula throughout the afternoon. A lightning strike from one of the storms killed a person in Siesta Key. A building fire was suspected to have been caused by another lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man and woman at the beach began to head for shelter as it started to rain. The man was struck by lightning, knocking back the woman back. The man was transported to a hospital, but was unable to be revived.

The atmosphere was favorable for showers and thunderstorms to develop along the east and west coast sea breezes. Due to the stronger west winds, the west coast sea breeze was dominant, and the main collision between the two sea breezes occurred closer to the east central Florida coast. Still, numerous showers and thunderstorms impacted much of east central Florida. One man was killed after being struck by lightning in Umatilla, while another man was injured after being struck by lightning in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per reports relayed via local broadcast media, an Orlando man working on the roof of his home was struck by lightning and injured. Two neighbors called 911 after finding the injured man laying on the ground behind his home, though he was conscious at the time. Orlando firefighters arrived at the man's home, which is located on Lake Arnold Place, near Primrose Drive just south of State Road 408. Firefighters transported the man to a local hospital as a trauma alert. The injured man survived his injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a commercial building in Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined, positively tilted trough aloft became a closed mid-level low pressure over north central Florida. This resulted in modest force for ascent across north central Florida, and also maintained a weak surface trough across north-central Florida, which helped to decrease the strong west/southwest flow, allowing the east coast sea breeze to develop in the afternoon. Moisture was plentiful across the area with Precipitable Water values around 1.50 - 1.70 inches. With diurnal heating, numerous showers and thunderstorms were able to develop along and a head of the east coast sea breeze. Once the initial round of storms got going, numerous outflow boundaries helped to create additional storms across the area. One storm in eastern Seminole County became severe where it produced quarter-sized hail. Another storm in Brevard County also became severe and it produced a 55 knot gust along the intracoastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public relayed video via social media from Chuluota showing mainly dime to nickel sized hail with a few quarter-sized hail stones mixed in. The same person also sent a video showing strong winds blowing over a trampoline. Those winds are estimated to be around 55 mph.

Moisture was plentiful across the area with Precipitable Water values around 1.50 - 1.70 inches. With diurnal heating, numerous showers and thunderstorms were able to develop along and a head of the east coast sea breeze. Once the initial round of storms got going, numerous outflow boundaries helped to create additional storms across the area. One storm in eastern Seminole County became severe where it produced quarter-sized hail. Another storm in Brevard County also became severe and it produced a 55 knot gust along the intracoastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public relayed a photo via social media showing nickel-sized hail in Taft, west of the Orlando International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined, positively tilted trough aloft became a closed mid-level low pressure over north central Florida. This resulted in modest force for ascent across north central Florida, and also maintained a weak surface trough across north-central Florida, which helped to decrease the strong west/southwest flow, allowing the east coast sea breeze to develop in the afternoon. Moisture was plentiful across the area with Precipitable Water values around 1.50 - 1.70 inches. With diurnal heating, numerous showers and thunderstorms were able to develop along and a head of the east coast sea breeze. Once the initial round of storms got going, numerous outflow boundaries helped to create additional storms across the area. One storm in eastern Seminole County became severe where it produced quarter-sized hail. Another storm in Brevard County also became severe and it produced a 55 knot gust along the intracoastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force wind tower 1007 measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central Florida was wedged between a high pressure ridge axis to the south, and a weak low pressure trough to the north. This resulted in strong west flow in the low-levels across east central Florida, and allowed deep moisture to remain in place across the area with Precipitable Water values near 2 inches. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours, mainly along the quickly progressing west coast sea breeze. The strong west winds delayed the development of the east coast sea breeze by a few hours, but by mid-afternoon the sea breeze had developed in Brevard County. Showers and thunderstorms began to develop along the slowly advancing sea breeze. Further south, in Indian River County, a thunderstorm there produced a northward moving outflow boundary that pushed into Brevard County and interacted with the sea breeze. This caused enhanced lift just along the sea breeze over Melbourne, and a severe thunderstorm quickly developed. This storm produced a severe wind gust in Melbourne Beach. In Port Orange, a home was destroyed after being struck by lightning and catching fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per a report received from a local broadcast media: a Port Orange home caught fire and was destroyed after being struck by lightning. The home located on Stewart Avenue, north of Taylor Road, was fully engulfed by flames when fire fighters arrived on scene. The blaze began in the garage area of the home and burned two vehicles before spreading to the rest of the property. The homeowner was working in his shed in the backyard when the lightning strike occurred, though he was uninjured. When Florida Power and Light inspected the home after the blaze, they found the plastic meter was melted and hanging off, another sign the home was struck by lightning. Unfortunately, the owner's two pet cats were killed in the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed in the Eastpoint area during the late afternoon of June 24th.  It quickly spread from the woods to nearby homes as strong northerly outflow from a thunderstorm to the north moved through the area with wind gusts of 25-30 mph.  According to emergency management, the fire destroyed 36 homes with an additional 4 homes sustaining minor damage.  There were 184 people who requested assistance through the Red Cross.  The fire grew to 950 acres.  It was 95 percent contained by the morning of June 26th.  The loss of property is estimated to be at least $800,000 according to Property Appraiser Rhonda Skipper.

Flood watch was in effect due to above normal moisture in place across the region ahead of a very strong, long wave trough storm system over the MS River Valley. The associated warm front was draped along the FL-GA stateline, which provide a focus for widespread heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily rainfall of 4.25 inches was measured near Fort McCoy since early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season frontal boundary moved slowly south across north Florida, while south of the boundary strong westerly flow was observed across central Florida. Deep moisture was in place across the area, and with cool temperatures aloft and daytime heating conditions became favorable for the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms in the afternoon. Some showers and storms formed across the interior counties early in the afternoon and pushed east towards the coast. One storm became severe as it moved across northern Brevard County where it produced wind damage at the Titusville Airport, along with small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was offshore of the SE GA Atlantic coast with an upper level low along the FL Gulf Coast. The upper level low lifted northward through the day, and combined with low level forcing, instability and high moisture produced strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 year old male was struck by lightning on  his front porch along Acadia Street in Keystone Heights, near Lake Geneva. The time of the event was based on lightning data. Shed was burned down which caused over $1M in damage to vintage cars and  motorcycles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The interaction between a strengthening low pressure system in the eastern Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Bermuda High caused south winds to develop across the Florida peninsula. These winds brought a surge of moisture from the Caribbean across the state of Florida. A weak inverted trough/low pressure system developed in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, this in turn increased the low-level shear across much of east central Florida. Widespread showers and thunderstorms were ongoing throughout the day. One thunderstorm just offshore the Brevard County Coast became strong and developed a mesocyclone. This storm produced a waterspout that moved onshore and produced minor damage on Merritt Island. A second storm over inland Martin County developed a mesocyclone and produced a weak tornado, resulting in minor damage near Indiantown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following examination of photos and video received from local broadcast media, NWS Melbourne confirmed an EF-0 tornado affected the River Palms Mobile Home Park on Merritt Island as a waterspout moved onshore from the Banana River. The tornado produced minor damage to a few mobile homes, mainly detaching awnings from trailers. Winds were estimated between 60 and 70 mph. No injuries or fatalities occurred as a result of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was draped over SE GA with a dominant east coast sea breeze drifting inland over a moist and unstable airmass. A severe storm impacted Marion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines near Westport High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a weak frontal boundary across Central Florida lead to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms across South Florida. Strong storms that developed across Palm Beach County during the evening hours produced small hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WPTV relayed a report and shared photos of a fence that was knocked over and a gazebo that was blown over with a thunderstorm that moved through the area near the intersection of Hypoluxo Road and Military Trail. Time is estimated via radar and concurrent observations. Radar estimated winds of 60 mph with nearby observations recording gusts of 45 to 50 mph. Pea sized hail was also reported in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level boundary was draped across north-central FL roughly from Suwanee to Marion counties with high moisture content and an unstable airmass pumping over the boundary from the Gulf of Mexico under WSW flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down along 700 N State Road 21.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey determined that a severe thunderstorm along a band of convection spawned a brief tornado over rural northwest Martin County. The EF-0 tornado, with estimated winds of 70-80 mph, caused minor damage to a screen porch and damaged roof shingles on a two-story home. The tornado also ripped a portions of a roof and south-facing wall from an adjacent barn and uprooted a large tree. Debris was carried northward from the impact area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: By 3 pm, a CoCoRaHS observer about 5 miles SSE of Marineland measured 4.79 inches in 90 minutes. At 3:21 pm, the EM reported multiple streets were flooded in NW Palm Coast. At 3:33 pm, a spotter in Palm Coast reported about 8 inches of water covered Pritchard Drive. At 4 pm, the public about 5 miles SSW of Marineland measured 5.13 inches of rainfall between 2 pm and 4 pm. At 4:27 pm, the EM reported Barrington Drive in Palm Coast was not passable due to flooding. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning storms once again developed along the east coast with the Atlantic seabreeze, with numerous storms across the interior and Gulf coast as seabreezed moved west and collided with the Gulf breeze along the Gulf coast during the afternoon hours. Shower and storm development along these boundaries was enhanced by a lingering upper level low across the northern Florida and the eastern Gulf of Mexico, allowing several strong storms to develop. Hail was reported in Collier County during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Florida peninsula experienced strong south to southeast flow with Subtropical Storm Alberto far to the west and a strong high pressure over the western Atlantic. These south winds allowed deep tropical moisture from the Caribbean to move across the Florida peninsula. Some mid-level dry air wrapped itself around the eastern side of Alberto's circulation, which decreased cloud cover across parts of central Florida allowing for daytime heating and instability to increase. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and moved towards the coast. One storm became severe in Martin County where it produced a short-lived, weak tornado (landspout).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per reports, photos and video received from Martin County Fire Rescue, various broadcast media, and social media posts, NWS Melbourne confirmed that a very weak, short-lived tornado (landspout) occurred near the intersection of SE Geraldine Street and SE Railway Avenue in Port Salerno. The tornado, which lasted only a few seconds, was rated as EF-0 (winds near 60 mph). The landspout caused a trampoline to become airborne, where it got stuck in power lines atop a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major Hurricane Michael made landfall near Mexico Beach, FL around 1 PM CDT on October 10, 2018 with estimated maximum winds on 155 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon made landfall just west of the Alabama-Mississippi at 1015 PM CDT on September 4, 2018 with estimated maximum sustained winds of 70 mph. Gordon then tracked northwest into portions of inland southeast Mississippi late on the night of September 4th into the early morning of September 5th. ||Gordon produced sustained tropical storm force winds Greene and George counties in Mississippi, coastal Alabama, as well as Escambia County in Florida. The strongest winds were confined to the immediate coast, with Dauphin Island experiencing gusts to hurricane force in the northern eyewall. Wind gusts to hurricane force were reported on Dauphin Island with sustained tropical storm force winds reported as far north as the |Mobile Regional Airport. Some homes on Dauphin Island experienced roof and siding damage. Isolated to scattered down trees and power outages were reported in southern mobile county. Further east, tropical storm force winds, primarily in gusts, impacted areas of Baldwin county south of interstate 10 with the strongest winds along the immediate coast. Strong tropical storm force winds were felt on the Ft. Morgan peninsula where a few homes suffered some siding or roof damage. The steeple was blown off of the Romar Beach Baptist church in Orange Beach. Some trees were downed and scattered power outages were reported. In Escambia County, FL, tropical storm force wind gusts impacted the southern half of the county, especially in the Pensacola metro and the immediate coast. Sustained tropical storm force winds were reported at Pensacola NAS. One fatality was reported near Pensacola when a tree fell onto a mobile home and killed a small child. In Greene and George counties in Mississippi, tropical storm force winds downed trees and power lines, particularly in the Merrill and Richton communities. One home in Richton was significantly damaged by a downed tree. ||Storm surge inundation peaked between 2 to 3 feet across coastal Alabama and Escambia County, FL. This resulted in flooding of typically low lying areas of Mobile and Baldwin Counties, including the west end of Dauphin Island and the US Highway 90 Causeway. The Fort Pickens area in Florida experienced significant coastal flooding and beach erosion. ||Rainfall totals of 6-12 inches were recorded to the east of the center, primarily over Baldwin, Washington, Choctaw, Clarke, Escamba AL, Escambia FL, and Santa Rosa FL. The highest total measured was 12.45 inches 7 miles ENE of Gulf Breeze, FL. This resulted in areas of flash flooding, along with some significant river flooding. The Styx River reached major flood stage while the Fish River reached moderate flood stage.

The strong onshore flow along the Gulf coast also lead to several areas of beach erosion and minor flooding along the Collier County coast.

A strong cold front moved southeast through the eastern Gulf of Mexico and Florida Peninsula on the 20th, preceded by a squall line. The storms produced 4 tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts, in addition to strong gradient winds behind the front on the 21st. The strong onshore winds also produced coastal flooding along the coast and moderate to major beach erosion from Pinellas County to Lee County. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS/CO-OPS site at Clearwater Beach (CWBF1) recorded a 62 knot gradient wind gust. The 1,200 foot Redington Long Pier collapsed in several sections due to high surf and crashing waves.  The pier was 56-years-old was closed in February of 2018 due to safety concerns from damage from 2017 Hurricane Irma. Damage estimate unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Storm Prediction Center issued an Enhanced Risk of severe weather for most of east central Florida. A strengthening low pressure system was moving across the southeastern U.S. with a strong cold front extending southwest across the Gulf of Mexico. A potent jet stream moved towards Florida with winds of  145 mph, at the same time a low-level jet situated along the cold front moved over the Florida peninsula with winds of 55-70 mph. The rapid increase and turning of winds with height in the atmosphere led to very high wind shear over Florida. Also, a surge of moisture and daytime heating led to an increase in instability over east central Florida that aided strong to severe thunderstorm development. By early morning, a squall line of thunderstorms began to move onshore in west central Florida and quickly push east across the peninsula. A severe thunderstorm embedded within the line caused damage to a home in Leesburg. In the early afternoon, as the line approached the Brevard County coast, a couple of severe wind gusts were reported west of Cape Canaveral. Lastly, during the late afternoon strong winds caused a tree to fall over onto a shed in Stuart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake County Emergency Management reported an aluminum porch roof was blown off a residential structure. Photos were later shared via social media by local broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Storm Prediction Center issued an Enhanced Risk of severe weather for most of east central Florida. A strengthening low pressure system was moving across the southeastern U.S. with a strong cold front extending southwest across the Gulf of Mexico. A potent jet stream moved towards Florida with winds of  145 mph, at the same time a low-level jet situated along the cold front moved over the Florida peninsula with winds of 55-70 mph. The rapid increase and turning of winds with height in the atmosphere led to very high wind shear over Florida. Also, a surge of moisture and daytime heating led to an increase in instability over east central Florida that aided strong to severe thunderstorm development. By early morning, a squall line of thunderstorms began to move onshore in west central Florida and quickly push east across the peninsula. A severe thunderstorm embedded within the line caused damage to a home in Leesburg. In the early afternoon, as the line approached the Brevard County coast, a couple of severe wind gusts were reported west of Cape Canaveral. Lastly, during the late afternoon strong winds caused a tree to fall over onto a shed in Stuart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force wind tower 1007 measured a peak wind gust of 50 knots from the west-southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter weather event occurred across the area on the 16th into the 17th across the Gulf Coast. This marks only the 2nd time in recorded history that two measurable snow events occurred in one winter in Mobile. With low temperatures in the 20s, the winter accumulation resulted in very hazardous traveling conditions, particularly over bridges and overpasses. Several roads were closed throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1.2 inches in Pace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly occurred near Waterford Lakes in Orlando. Minor wind damage impacted a few homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media forwarded a report of a screened pool enclosure destroyed at a home on Wildlife Place in Orlando. Delayed reports from Orange County Fire Rescue indicated two nearby homes on Brown Trout Circle also experienced some minor structural damage. Residents reportedly witnessed the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary drapped across the region with enough tropical moisture across the region allowed thunderstorms to develop. Heavy rainfall from training of storms over the same region caused some minor flooding in West Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of flooded roadways from broadcast media partners in West Palm Beach. Many roadways were closed including N Australian Avenue from Banyan Blvd to Palm Beach Lakes Blvd. This included an area near an outlet mall at Palm Beach Lakes and Village Boulevards, as well as neighborhoods in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in the afternoon along the sea breeze and moved towards the interior. As outflow boundaries and sea breeze boundaries interacted a funnel cloud formed over Miami Dade County in a wall cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employees witness funnel cloud form out of rotating wall cloud just south of NWS Miami office. The funnel did not extend far out of the cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and instability across South Florida allowed thunderstorms to develop along the east coast sea breeze in the afternoon. Sea breeze and outflow boundaries continued to allow storms to develop over the same region causing flooding in Miami-Dade County and a Tornado in Broward. These storms also produced frequent to excessive lightning causing fatalities and damage across the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple photos shared from broadcast media showed a tornado in western Broward County. The tornado occurred west of Griffin Road over the Everglades lasting a few minutes. There was no damage reported as it stayed over the open Everglades.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee called to report that the express lanes on I-95 had been closed due to flooding from heavy rainfall with thunderstorms training over the same region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly wind flow with tropical moisture pumped in across the region allowed for a stormy weather pattern across South Florida. Afternoon thunderstorms developed along the east coast in the afternoon and evening. One of these storms produced a funnel cloud over downtown Miami-Dade.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter called to report a funnel cloud associated with a thunderstorm near Biscayne Blvd and NE 64th Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and downed a power pole along the south side of NW 7th Street near NW 15th Avenue in Miami. the power pole fell across NW 7th Street, resulting in the closure of both the street and the third base parking garage at Marlins Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed across the western east coast suburbs and the interior during a typical afternoon summertime pattern across South Florida. One of these storms produced a funnel cloud in Pembroke Pines in western Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple spotters and an off-duty NWS employee reported a funnel cloud halfway down to the ground. Photos from the off-duty NWS employee indicate funnel was roughly halfway down to the ground. The funnel cloud was in Pembroke Pines just to the west of I-75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of tropical moisture and steep lapse rates with ample instability across the region. Thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze and convection impacted much of the east coast of South Florida. One of theses storms produced a brief weak tornado that caused some damage in the city of Sweetwater in Miami Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey, along with eyewitness reports, indicated that a tornado briefly touched down in Miami near the intersection of Southwest 8th Street and Southwest 103rd Avenue. Minor damage to trees, mailboxes, street signs, canopies, and basketball hoops occurred as the tornado moved east-southeast along 900 block of Southwest 101st Avenue and Southwest 100th Court, before the tornado became a waterspout over northwest corner of a large retention pond and lifted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple media outlets reported that 2 men were working in a wooded area when a tree near them was struck by lightning sending the current into them. They both were then transported to local area hospitals where one man was deceased and the other survived his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance with colder air aloft allowed for strong scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. With weak low-level wind flow this allowed for storms over the Atlantic to produce a waterspout. One of these waterspouts moved ashore becoming a tornado near Juno Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources including spotter call and video on social media showed a waterspout that made landfall a few dozen yards south of Juno Beach Pier at 1100AM EDT. The tornado lifted shortly after making landfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high October astronomical tides, King tides, in combination with the strong winds and minor surge from Hurricane Michael in the Gulf of Mexico brought a couple days of minor saltwater flooding along both the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos received via social media show very minor tidal inundation at Sewell Park along the Miami River near NW 17th Avenue in Miami.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high October astronomical tides, King tides, in combination with the strong winds and minor surge from Hurricane Michael in the Gulf of Mexico brought a couple days of minor saltwater flooding along both the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported saltwater flooding of 4 to 6 inches at the intersection of Brickell Ave and Coral Way due to high astronomical tides. Other location in Brickell had 2 to 4 inches of saltwater flooding near drains and curbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy easterly winds brought an elevated rip current risk along the Atlantic beaches of South Florida for several days during the end of October. Several visitors to the beach were suspected to be victims of rip currents during the late morning low tide along Miami Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami-Dade Fire Rescue reports that around 830 AM EDT, a man and woman were reported in distress in the water near 6th Street on Miami Beach. Both person were pulled from the water by Ocean Rescue and taken to the hospital where the man later died. There were numerous reports of rip currents in the vicinity throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds associated with fast moving squalls crossed Lake Monroe, causing choppy seas. A boat containing two men who were fishing capsized around 1330LST. One man fell from the boat and floated away. He was found deceased the following day. The second man clung to the side of the boat overnight and was rescued around noon the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large circulation around Hurricane Michael located near the western tip of Cuba combined with high pressure to the north caused quick-moving showers with gusty winds to move west across central Florida from the Atlantic. As these showers moved over inland lakes, wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph occurred. A boat containing two men who were fishing on Lake Monroe capsized due to choppy seas created by the gusty winds. One man, who was wearing a non-secured life vest, drifted away from the boat and was found deceased the following day. The other man retrieved his lift vest while hanging onto the side of the boat and was rescued nearly 24 hours later. He was transported to a local hospital, suffering from hypothermia and exhaustion and was later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was moving across the Florida peninsula as a strong mid to upper level trough pushed across the southeast United States. Ahead of the front, a fast moving pre-frontal band of showers and thunderstorms became well organized over the eastern Gulf of Mexico before pushing onshore near the Tampa Bay area. Daytime heating and sufficient deep layer moisture meant high instability was present over the peninsula to sustain strong to severe thunderstorms. As one cluster of thunderstorms moved into Lake County, it became severe and produced wind damage near Minneola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed reports of several trees down on power lines on Buckhill Road, just north of Minneola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm near the lower Florida Keys produced a damaging lightning strike to a residence on Ramrod Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a residence along Barbados Lane on Ramrod Key, resulting in roof and electrical system damage.  A neighboring residence also experienced electrical system damage.  Report relayed by social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong cold front. These thunderstorms produced high winds and an isolated tornado which caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A food services building and perimeter fence were damaged at the Okaloosa Correctional Facility. Based on an analysis of radar data and pictures from emergency management, the damage was the likely result of a very brief EF-0 touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the northern Gulf along with a northward moving warm front combined to generate thunderstorms across the area. A few of the storms become severe and produced wind damage along with a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived EF-0 tornado touched down in wooded area southwest of the intersection of Gulf Breeze Parkway and Woodland Beach Road. This tornado then moved northeast at around 30 mph, and passed just west of the intersection before lifting north-northeast of the intersection. There was a small area of tree damage in the wooded area where several trees were leaning and a couple uprooted. Two vehicles near the intersection suffered damage with a window shattered in each vehicle from flying debris. Several signs near the intersection were bent or knocked to the ground. Some straight line wind damage also occurred in the area, most notable 1.3 miles east of the tornado track where playground equipment was thrown and destroyed by 70 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours on the 2nd. A squall-line associated with the front produced widespread damaging wind gusts along with multiple tornadoes embedded within the fast-moving line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damage was observed in two mobile home parks, and a condominium roof was torn off in addition to numerous downed trees and branches. One minor injury was reported in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local amateur radio operator had the roof off of his mobile home blown off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred near Land O Lakes which condemned two homes due to significant roof damage. In addition, there were numerous downed trees in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of strong to isolated severe storms moved across NE FL during the afternoon. Strong speed shear and surface based instability produced damaging winds in the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Datil Pepper Road. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of strong to isolated severe storms moved across NE FL during the afternoon. Strong speed shear and surface based instability produced damaging winds in the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Oceanside Blvd in Flagler Beach. The time of damage was based on radar. Numerous spotter and mesonet networks measured wind gusts between 40-45 mph when the storm passed over Flagler Beach. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure east of the region and high pressure north of the area created strong onshore flow. The persistent onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides created moderate tidal flooding around high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mayport C-Man station reported water level at 2.07 ft above MHHW around high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure east of the region and high pressure north of the area created strong onshore flow. The persistent onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides created moderate tidal flooding around high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water level peaked at 2.37 ft above MHHW at the Fernandina Beach C-man station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure east of the region and high pressure north of the area created strong onshore flow. The persistent onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides created moderate tidal flooding around high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A1A flooded along the Matanzas River in historic St. Augustine. The nearest tide gage along the Tolomoto River measured water level 3.67 ft MHHW as of 8:54 am, reaching moderate flooding thresholds. Thus far, this level tied the 9th highest historic crest for the site, previously set on 11/14/2012.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure east of the region and high pressure north of the area created strong onshore flow. The persistent onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides created moderate tidal flooding around high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding was observed along Beach Blvd and the Intracoastal Waterway. Water was over the road and across parking lots near Beach Marine. Observations were consistent with strong local Nor'easter events rising water levels into minor flood stage to low end moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure east of the region and high pressure north of the area created strong onshore flow. The persistent onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides created moderate tidal flooding around high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over-topped older docks in Palm Coast with high tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding occurred at morning high tide on 11/23 around 11:30 am and again on 11/24 at 12:30 pm along Hopkins Creek in Neptune Beach just off of the Intracoastal Waterway. Water topped the Kings Road Causeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal storms developed across NE FL ahead with surface based CAPE near 1000 J/kg and bulk 0-6 km shear around 45 kts with elevated steep lapse rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to pea size hail was reported in Crescent Beach.
